rootCause: kube-proxy iptables rules out of sync, DNAT not properly forwarding traffic from LB to backend pods
solution: 1. Test external IP and nodeport separately (nc -vz). 2. If both fail internally, check application health and iptables rules. 3. Restart kube-proxy pods to resync iptables. 4. Check NSG rules. 5. If internal works but external fails, check VIP/DIP availability and engage networking team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/AKS Network Troubleshooting Methodology/[TSG] K8s LoadBalancer Service

symptom: Applications in AKS cannot reach external IPs — SNAT port exhaustion on load balancer
rootCause: Insufficient allocated SNAT ports for outbound connections through Azure Load Balancer
solution: Check SNAT metrics via Applens detector or Jarvis dashboard. Increase allocated outbound ports: az aks update --load-balancer-outbound-ports. Ref: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/load-balancer-standard#configure-the-allocated-outbound-ports
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/AKS Network Troubleshooting Methodology/[TSG] K8s LoadBalancer Service

symptom: AKS cluster issues caused by custom NSG rules — load balancer access blocked, control plane communication interrupted, egress traffic denied
rootCause: Custom Network Security Group rules on NIC or subnet level blocking required AKS traffic (required egress ports/FQDNs, LB health probe 168.63.129.16, control plane communication)
solution: 1. Find associated NSGs in ASC (check both NIC and subnet level). 2. Validate security rules allow required egress per https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/limit-egress-traffic. 3. Check for custom rules with deny actions that may block required communication. Default NSG rules are generally OK.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/AKS Network Troubleshooting Methodology/[TSG] Network Security Group (NSG)

symptom: Private AKS cluster — unable to connect to API server due to DNS lookup failure for private FQDN
rootCause: Private DNS zone not properly linked to the VNet, or DNS record for API server missing/incorrect in the private DNS zone
solution: 1. Check DNS zone linked to VNet: az network private-dns link vnet list. 2. Check DNS A record exists: az network private-dns record-set a list. 3. Verify record points to correct private endpoint IP. 4. If using custom DNS, ensure conditional forwarding to 168.63.129.16 for privatelink zone.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/AKS Network Troubleshooting Methodology/[TSG] PrivateLink Cluster

symptom: Private AKS cluster — unable to connect to API server due to timeout, DNS resolves but connection hangs
rootCause: Private endpoint or network interface misconfiguration — private endpoint NIC not in correct subnet, privateLinkServiceConnection not approved, or network-level blocking
solution: 1. Check private endpoint: az network private-endpoint list. 2. Verify NIC is in cluster subnet and status is Approved. 3. Test connectivity: nc -vz <apiserver-fqdn> 443 from node or VM in same subnet. 4. If networking issue, engage Azure Networking team. CCP logs: FrontEndContextActivity / AsyncContextActivity at aks.kusto.windows.net
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/AKS Network Troubleshooting Methodology/[TSG] PrivateLink Cluster

symptom: AKS cluster operations (upgrade, scale, create) fail with HTTP 429 OperationNotAllowed / TooManyRequests, or experience connection timeouts/resets/task-canceled errors; ARM response headers show x-ms-ratelimit-remaining values depleted
rootCause: Subscription exceeds ARM-level rate limits (15000 reads/hr, 1200 writes/hr per FrontDoor instance). Common causes: monitoring software making excessive GET calls, too many concurrent users, or automated scripts constantly scanning the subscription. ARM throttling identified by failureCause=Gateway in HttpIncomingRequests.
solution: 1) Identify throttling source via Kusto: query HttpIncomingRequests in armprod for 429 status codes; failureCause=Gateway means ARM throttling, failureCause=Service means RP throttling. 2) Reduce API call rate: run automated scripts less frequently, scope queries to needed resources. 3) Use Azure Resource Graph for read-heavy workloads. 4) ARM/RP rate limits are global and cannot be increased per-subscription. 5) Use clientApplicationId and operationName to identify which app/user is causing excessive calls.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/Cluster Management/429 Throttling

symptom: AKS operations fail with 429 from individual Resource Provider (e.g., VMSS, Network) visible in HttpOutgoingRequests; failureCause=Service in HttpIncomingRequests; may also see HTTP 502/504 Gateway Timeout from RP endpoints
rootCause: Individual Resource Provider (RP) rate limiting, independent from ARM throttling. RP endpoints may be overloaded with traffic or enforce per-subscription limits. RP limits are often not publicly documented (except Network and Storage). RP throttling is the most common form of throttling encountered.
solution: 1) Check HttpOutgoingRequests in armprod Kusto, filter for httpStatusCode 429, examine hostName to identify which RP is throttling. 2) For systemic RP overload (not per-subscription), the supporting team should open ICM with the RP dev team. 3) Also check for HTTP 502/504 with failureCause=service indicating RP timeout. 4) Customer must reduce API call rate to the affected RP. 5) RP throttling config can be found by searching ThrottlingConfig in the One codebase.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/Cluster Management/429 Throttling

symptom: AKS node pool creation or scaling fails with error: ConfigurationVersion 'v0.xxxxxxxx.x' is not supported for AgentBaker; error persists even after deleting/recreating nodepool, specifying latest NodeImageVersion, or using ARM/Terraform
rootCause: Internal AgentBaker ConfigurationVersion stored with agent pool profile has been retired by the service. This is NOT a VM image issue. Occurs on long-lived clusters, snapshot-based pools, or clusters without recent control plane Kubernetes version upgrades. AgentBaker validates configurationVersion before evaluating node image.
solution: 1) Preferred: Upgrade AKS control plane Kubernetes version to force regeneration of agent pool profiles and rebind to a supported configurationVersion. 2) Alternative: If customer won't upgrade and has at least one healthy nodepool, run 'az aks nodepool update --resource-group <rg> --cluster-name <cluster> --name <existing-pool>' to reconcile metadata. 3) If all mitigations fail, create ICM to EEE/AKS RP team for service-side reconciliation. GitHub tracking: https://github.com/Azure/AgentBaker/issues/6229
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Create/AgentBaker NodePool ConfigVersionError

symptom: AKS cluster create or start operation fails with AKSCapacityError or AKSCapacityHeavyUsage: 'Creating a new cluster or start cluster is unavailable at this time in region <Region>'
rootCause: The region is capacity constrained for AKS. AKS has a Constrained Regions Plan that purposefully slows down creates to prevent existing clusters from experiencing runtime errors. Regions go through Green/Yellow/Orange/Red phases based on underlay utilization. Non-EA (free/Azure Plan) subscriptions are throttled first. QatarCentral is extremely constrained with no new cores until 2026.
solution: 1) Primary: use an alternate region (check ASC Capacity Allocation Recommender); 2) Retry later (especially end-of-day when capacity is reclaimed); 3) Try different cluster type (public vs private may use different underlays); 4) Use EA subscription (less restricted); 5) For QatarCentral whitelist requests: create ICM assigned to Annie Bubinski with subscription IDs and cluster count; 6) For urgent cases: collect business justification and reach out to AKS EEE. Kusto: check underlay capacity via FrontEndContextActivity operationName=PutManagedClusterHandler.PUT, look for 'underlay in region' message. Dashboard: AKS Underlay Capacity (Geneva).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Create/AKSCapacityError or AKSCapacityHeavyUsage Cluster create blocked in constrained region

symptom: AKS nodepool creation fails with InvalidParameter DiskControllerTypeMismatchBetweenOsAndVMScaleSetSize when using VM size Standard_E112ibds_v5
rootCause: VM Size Standard_E112ibds_v5 explicitly only supports NVMe DiskControllerType, but the AKS node OS image does not specify a diskControllerType, so it defaults to SCSI. CRP rejects the mismatch: 'The Virtual Machine Scale Set with VM size Standard_E112ibds_v5 cannot boot with OS image or disk'. This is not an AKS-specific bug but a VM SKU + image compatibility issue.
solution: Use VM size Standard_E112iads_v5 instead (supports SCSI, less restrictive disk controller requirements). Verify SKU disk controller support via https://aka.ms/azure-compute-skus. Reference: Ebdsv5 series uses NVMe interface for Gen2 (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/ebdsv5-ebsv5-series).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Create/DiskControllerTypeMismatchBetweenOsAndVMScaleSetSize_with_size_Standard_E112ibds_v5

symptom: AKS Machine API (LIST/GET Machines) returns NotFound with GetAgentPool_NotFound or NoRegisteredProviderFound error
rootCause: Two root causes: 1) GetAgentPool_NotFound: agentpool not yet registered in DB (race condition during creation) or incorrect agentpool name in target URI; 2) NoRegisteredProviderFound: specific REST API version used by Machine API implementation is not supported in the target region.
solution: For GetAgentPool_NotFound: verify agentpool exists via List AgentPools API or GET Agentpool; ensure correct agentpool name in URI path parameter. For NoRegisteredProviderFound: no client-side mitigation available — notify AKS dev team immediately. Machine API does not have async operations. Kusto: FrontEndQoSEvents where operationName == 'ListMachinesHandler.GET' or 'GetMachineHandler.GET'; FrontEndContextActivity for detailed operation context.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Create/GET and LIST Machine API Troubleshooting

symptom: AKS cluster/nodepool CRUD operations (create, update, delete) fail with NetworkingInternalOperationError: 'An unexpected error occurred while processing the network profile of the VM'
rootCause: Failures in updating VMSS underlying Fabric or Network resources in Azure Compute. Related error codes include RnmServiceDeletionCleanupTimeout and NrpInternalException/InternalServerError. This is an Azure Compute/Network internal issue, not AKS-specific. Detailed investigation reference: http://aka.ms/CCSupNetworkingInternalError
solution: 1) For delete operations: try manually deleting the underlying VMSS resource directly via 'az vmss delete'; the issue may auto-resolve after some time so retrying helps; 2) Collect VMSS resource ID and failed CRP operation ID from ASI; 3) Engage VM/VMSS delivery pod team via collaboration task or EEE AzureRT team via IcM. Check CRP logs via ASI CRP VMSS search for detailed error context.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/AKS CRUD operation failing with NetworkingInternalOperationError

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 1 on Linux. Two variants: (A) cloud-init fails with 'Failed to create user' and 'useradd: invalid user name' in /var/log/cloud-init.log; (B) AKSLinuxExtension fails with 'dpkg is locked by another process' and 'Installing node-problem-detector failed'.
rootCause: Variant A: Linux admin username exceeds 32 characters. The useradd command rejects usernames >32 chars. Cluster may have been created before frontend validation was added, or customer used REST API directly. Variant B: dpkg process locked by concurrent Linux package updates. Old/unsupported AKS versions with outdated node images trigger concurrent apt updates causing dpkg lock contention.
solution: Variant A: Change admin username to <32 characters: az aks create --admin-username <short_name>. Use Kusto query on FrontEndContextActivity to verify current username length. Variant B: Upgrade cluster to recent supported version and update node image. For both: collect guest logs via InspectIaaSDisk with AKS manifest, check /var/log/cloud-init.log and extension.log.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS Windows node provisioning fails with exit code 33/34/35 — WINDOWS_CSE_ERROR_DOWNLOAD failures for CSE package, Kubernetes binaries, or CNI package from acs-mirror.azureedge.net
rootCause: Windows nodes cannot reach acs-mirror.azureedge.net to download CSE scripts, Kubernetes binaries, or Azure CNI packages. Caused by NVA/firewall/proxy. HTTP proxy is not supported for Windows nodepools.
solution: 1. Confirm customer is not using HTTP proxy (not supported for Windows nodepools). 2. Check UDR/firewall/NVA for blocks to acs-mirror.azureedge.net. 3. Verify all required outbound per Azure Global required network rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 50 (ERR_OUTBOUND_CONN_FAIL) — VNET address range overlaps with public IP (e.g. 13.0.0.0/8) causing routing failure to mcr.microsoft.com
rootCause: Customer deployed AKS in a custom VNET with address range overlapping public IPs (e.g. 13.0.0.0/8). Azure endpoint resolves to an IP in that range, which VM treats as private/local, causing routing failure.
solution: 1. Verify VNET address space does not overlap with public IP ranges. 2. Change VNET to RFC 1918 private ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x). 3. Test with curl from the node.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 50 — Azure VNET encryption enabled with firewall/NVA blocks outbound traffic inspection
rootCause: Azure VNET encryption is enabled and a firewall/NVA between AKS nodes and required endpoints cannot inspect or route encrypted traffic. VNET encryption is not supported with firewall/NVA in the path.
solution: Disable VNET encryption if a firewall or NVA is present between AKS nodes and the internet/required Azure endpoints.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 84 (ERR_GPU_DRIVERS_START_FAIL) — NVIDIA GPU drivers fail to install or start
rootCause: NVIDIA GPU drivers did not correctly install or start. Common cause: customer did not follow GPU documentation, used wrong installation method, or node image is incompatible.
solution: 1. Verify customer followed GPU documentation. 2. Check GPU driver installation method (automatic vs manual). 3. Run InspectIaaSDisk and check /var/log/nvidia-installer-xyz.log.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 99 (ERR_APT_UPDATE_TIMEOUT) — apt-get update times out during provisioning
rootCause: The custom script extension timed out running apt-get update. A Linux update package is taking too long, often due to network connectivity issues to package repositories or concurrent dpkg process.
solution: 1. Check /var/log/dpkg.log for time gaps. 2. Run InspectIaaSDisk with AKS manifest for cluster-provision.log. 3. Check endpoint connectivity. 4. Fix network configuration if needed. 5. SSH to node and run apt-get update to reproduce.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 100 (ERR_CSE_PROVISION_SCRIPT_NOT_READY_TIMEOUT) — /opt/azure/containers/provision.sh: No such file or directory
rootCause: Bootstrap provision script is missing. Most common cause: customer modified VMSS OS configuration custom Data (unsupported). Alternative: CRP/Fabric failure at Pre-Provisioning Service preventing ISO mount.
solution: 1. Check VMSS OS configuration custom data — if modified, that is unsupported. 2. Check Extensions page in ASC. 3. If custom data was not modified, escalate to CRP team as potential platform issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 124 (ERR_VHD_FILE_NOT_FOUND) — actually a timeout exit code masking underlying networking errors (50/51/52)
rootCause: Exit code 124 is the Linux timeout command exit code, not a genuine VHD error. The vmssCSE timeout wraps provisioning, so networking issues report as 124 instead of the real error code (50/51/52).
solution: 1. Treat as networking issue — same as exit codes 50/51/52. 2. Check firewall, DNS, NSG, UDR for outbound connectivity issues. 3. In rare cases may be cloud-init file write failure.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 50 (ERR_OUTBOUND_CONN_FAIL) or exit code 124 (ERR_VHD_FILE_NOT_FOUND, actually a timeout masking code 50/51/52). Node cannot reach mcr.microsoft.com:443 during VMSS scale-out or cluster create/upgrade.
rootCause: Node outbound connectivity to mcr.microsoft.com is blocked. Common causes: (1) NSG blocking outbound to port 443, (2) UDR forcing traffic through firewall/NVA that drops the traffic, (3) customer VNET using overlapping address range (e.g. 13.0.0.0/8) causing MCR IP to be treated as private, (4) AAAA/IPv6 records blocked by firewall, (5) VNet encryption enabled with firewall/NVA in path (encrypted traffic cannot be inspected), (6) DNS cannot resolve mcr.microsoft.com.
solution: 1. Run on node: curl -v --connect-timeout 10 --insecure --proxy-insecure https://mcr.microsoft.com/v2/ (or via az vmss run-command). 2. If curl hangs without 'Trying IP:443' line -> DNS resolution failure, check nslookup mcr.microsoft.com against each VNET DNS server. 3. If SSL_ERROR_SYSCALL -> proxy/firewall blocking TLS handshake. 4. Check if VNet encryption is enabled with NVA - disable VNet encryption if so. 5. Ensure NSG/UDR/firewall allows traffic per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/outbound-rules-control-egress. For exit code 124: treat as networking issue (50/51/52) - it is the timeout wrapper exit code.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 51 (ERR_K8S_API_SERVER_CONN_FAIL) - nc -vz <APIServer FQDN> 443 returns Connection timed out; or exit code 52 (ERR_K8S_API_SERVER_DNS_LOOKUP_FAIL) - nslookup <ClusterFqdn> fails to resolve.
rootCause: Exit code 51: API server unreachable from nodes. Causes: (1) authorized IP ranges missing outbound IP of firewall/NVA, (2) complex network topology (VPN GW + Palo Alto) where outbound IP not whitelisted, (3) cluster in Stopped state and last Start failed. Exit code 52: DNS cannot resolve cluster FQDN. For private clusters with custom DNS: (1) during initial create, private DNS zone not yet linked to VNET (expected, self-heals on subsequent PUT), (2) custom DNS misconfigured - conditional forwarding does not support subdomains for private link hostname.
solution: Exit code 51: (1) Check authorized IP ranges, add firewall outbound IP. (2) If cluster Stopped, retry Start (or Stop -> wait 30min -> Start). (3) Verify nc -vz <FQDN> 443. Exit code 52: (1) nslookup <ClusterFqdn> from node. (2) For private cluster + custom DNS: follow Hub & Spoke guide at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/private-clusters. (3) If conditional forwarding fails for subdomain, create identical record without subdomain in AKS-managed DNS zone.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 25 (ERR_MOBY_APT_LIST_TIMEOUT). Error: curl: (60) SSL certificate problem: unable to get local issuer certificate when reaching packages.microsoft.com.
rootCause: SSL inspection tool (e.g., ZScaler, corporate proxy) intercepts HTTPS connections to packages.microsoft.com and replaces the certificate with its own CA. The node CA trust store does not contain the proxy CA, so curl fails SSL verification - effectively a man-in-the-middle scenario.
solution: Bypass the SSL inspection tool (ZScaler/corporate proxy) for communications to packages.microsoft.com and other required AKS outbound FQDNs. If using AKS HTTP proxy feature, ensure the proxy CA certificate is included in the cluster trusted CA configuration. Verify: curl -v https://packages.microsoft.com/config/ubuntu/18.04/prod.list from node to check certificate chain.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS node provisioning fails with vmssCSE exit code 31 (ERR_K8S_DOWNLOAD_TIMEOUT), 41 (ERR_CNI_DOWNLOAD_TIMEOUT), or Windows exit codes 2/33/34/35. Common error: Cannot open: No such file or directory for kubernetes binaries, or Failed in downloading https://acs-mirror.azureedge.net/... Error: The remote name could not be resolved.
rootCause: NVA, firewall, or proxy blocking outbound access to acs-mirror.azureedge.net. Firewall may be allowlisting by IP instead of FQDN, and IP changes break connectivity. For Windows nodes: HTTP proxy is not supported on Windows nodepools and causes inconsistent network behaviors.
solution: 1. Verify connectivity: curl -v --connect-timeout 10 https://acs-mirror.azureedge.net/<path>. 2. Ensure firewall allowlists FQDN (not IP) per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/outbound-rules-control-egress. 3. For Windows: confirm HTTP proxy is NOT in use (not supported). 4. Check UDR/firewall/NVA/DMZ/proxy/VPN configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/CSE Exit Codes/vmssCSE failures during node provisioning

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with DeleteResourceGroupError / ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout — NSG in node resource group cannot be deleted, error contains Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Networking.Rnm.Contracts.Faults
rootCause: Backend services (ARM, NRP) became out-of-sync due to temporary outage or high load, resulting in a 'leaked' NSG object. One service reports the object absent while another has a to-be-deleted object blocking new operations. This is inherent to distributed systems with independent lifecycles; storage design prefers dangling objects over forced deletes to avoid data loss.
solution: No CSS self-service fix. Escalate via IcM to Cloudnet/NRP EEE team. Diagnostics: 1) Check AKS RP AsyncQoSEvents for failed delete correlationID, 2) Query FrontEndContextActivity/AsyncContextActivity with operationID for full error, 3) Attempt manual NSG delete to surface better error in ARM HttpIncomingRequests, 4) Query NRP FrontendOperationEtwEvent with correlationID from ARM for detailed error.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/AKS cluster cannot be deleted because NSG deletion fails with Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Networking.Rnm.Contracts.Faults

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with DeleteResourceGroupError and ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout - NSG (aks-agentpool-*-nsg) cannot be deleted, error Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Networking.Rnm.Contracts.Faults
rootCause: Azure backend services (NRP) out-of-sync: a leaked NSG object exists in one service layer but not another, blocking resource group deletion. Caused by transient outage or out-of-order writes in distributed backend services.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/AKS cluster cannot be deleted because NSG deletion fails with Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Networking.Rnm.Contracts.Faults
solution: No customer-ready mitigation. Escalate via IcM to Cloudnet/NRP (EEE) with diagnostic details. Use Kusto: (1) AKSprod.AsyncQoSEvents for failed delete operationID, (2) AKSprod.FrontEndContextActivity/AsyncContextActivity for full error, (3) ARMProd.HttpIncomingRequests for NSG delete correlation, (4) mdsnrp.FrontendOperationEtwEvent for NRP details.

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with PublicIPAddressCannotBeDeleted or similar errors — resources (LB, public IP) exist in NRP but are missing from ARM perspective, preventing resource group cleanup
rootCause: ARM bug: when customer performs a fast PUT on a network object after a previous DELETE, ARM honors the stale DELETE and removes the resource from ARM cache while it still exists in NRP/NFVRP. ARM and resource provider become permanently out-of-sync. Tracked by ARM team Bug 7832895.
solution: 1) Use ARM 'Get resource' Jarvis action to confirm resource missing from ARM but present in NRP. 2) Run ARM 'sync resource state' Jarvis action to recreate the missing resource in ARM. 3) After sync, customer retries delete — should succeed. Permanent fix pending ARM migration to CosmosDB (PBI 6463105). Kusto: query NRP FrontendOperationEtwEvent for failed LB/PIP delete details.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/AKS delete errors due to ARM cache issues

symptom: AKS node pool deletion fails with 'Cannot evict pod as it would violate the pod's disruption budget' / Too Many Requests error during drain — PDB minAvailable equals current healthy replicas, disruptionsAllowed is 0
rootCause: Pod Disruption Budget (PDB) is configured so that minAvailable equals total healthy pod count (or only one replica exists). During node pool delete, AKS must cordon+drain each node; if any pod cannot be evicted due to PDB violation (disruptionsAllowed=0), the drain fails and the entire nodepool deletion is blocked.
solution: 1) Identify blocking PDB: kubectl get pdb -n <namespace>, check DisruptionsAllowed=0. 2) Options: a) Scale up the workload so disruptions are allowed, b) Relax the PDB (lower minAvailable or increase maxUnavailable), c) Use --ignore-pdb flag: az aks nodepool delete --resource-group <rg> --cluster-name <cluster> --name <nodepool> --ignore-pdb (accepts risk of temporary workload disruption). Doc: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/aks/nodepool#az-aks-nodepool-delete
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/AgentPool delete with ignore PDB

symptom: AKS agentpool delete fails with FailToDeleteVMSS / DiskServiceInternalError — 'The operation is not permitted because there is a lease on the blob' targeting OS disk ID
rootCause: Known CRP-level race condition: while processing a VM disk's lease during VMSS deletion, CRP encounters a lease conflict on the OS disk blob. AKS retries VMSS deletion multiple times but the lease is not released. Tracked in AzureIaaSVM work item 78300.
solution: Requires manual mitigation from VMSS team. 1) Run Kusto query on azcrp/crp_allprod ApiQosEvent_nonGet for failed DELETE ops with resultCode=DiskServiceInternalError to get CRP operationId and correlationId. 2) Engage VMSS team via collaboration task or ICM to AzureRT/ScaleSet Service\VMSS. Provide: VMSS ID, CRP operation ID, CRP correlation ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/Agentpool delete operation failed with FailToDeleteVMSS DiskServiceInternalError

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with DNCCleanupServiceError / UnexpectedStatusFromDNCCleanup — 'expected dnccleanup to return a 404, got back 202 instead'
rootCause: DNC (Delegated Network Controller) cleanup service returns unexpected HTTP 202 instead of expected 404 during cluster deletion, indicating the DNC resource cleanup did not complete as expected. This is a transient backend issue in the DNC service.
solution: Requires PG mitigation. Open ICM referencing the internal DNC cleanup TSG: https://eng.ms/docs/cloud-ai-platform/azure-core/azure-networking/sdn-dbansal/azure-container-networking/azure-container-networking-tsgs/tsgs/aks/dnccleanup/dnccleanup. The error is marked retriable but typically requires PG intervention to resolve.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/Cluster delete operation failed with DNCCleanupServiceError

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with ResourceGroupDeletionBlocked or ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout — a managed resource in the node resource group cannot be deleted because it is referenced by a non-managed resource (e.g., custom NSG on cluster subnet, Private Link Service referencing managed LB, managed route table associated with non-managed subnet, managed IP used by external LB/Firewall, managed subnet used by non-managed VMs/VMSS)
rootCause: Customer created cross-references between AKS-managed infrastructure resources and non-managed resources. Modifying managed resources or adding non-managed resources in the node resource group is not supported. During cluster delete, AKS triggers node RG deletion, but Azure RP refuses to delete managed resources that are still referenced by external resources.
solution: Ask customer to remove the association/reference between the managed and non-managed resource, then retry cluster delete. Use ASI AKS Operation search (https://asi.azure.ms/services/AKS/pages/Operation%20Ids) to identify which specific managed resource and cross-reference is blocking deletion. Doc: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/aks/faq#why-are-two-resource-groups-created-with-aks-
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/Cluster delete operation failed with ResourceGroupDeletionBlocked ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout

symptom: AKS cluster delete fails with ResourceGroupDeletionBlocked or ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout — managed resource deletion fails with internal CRP/NRP error (e.g., Microsoft.Windows.Azure.GCM.ContractException, NetworkingInternalOperationError) rather than a cross-reference issue
rootCause: Internal error in Azure resource provider (CRP for compute resources like VMSS, NRP for network resources like LB/NSG/PIP) during managed resource deletion. Not caused by customer configuration but by transient or persistent RP-side issues.
solution: 1) Use ASI AKS Operation to identify the failing resource. 2) Query CRP (azcrp/crp_allprod ApiQosEvent_nonGet) or NRP logs for the failed DELETE operation details. 3) Try manual resource deletion (e.g., az vmss delete) as a quick mitigation — issue may auto-resolve. 4) If not resolved, escalate to owning RP team: EEE AzureRT for VM/VMSS failures, EEE Cloudnet for network failures — provide resource ID, CRP/NRP operation ID and correlation ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/CRUD/Delete/Cluster delete operation failed with ResourceGroupDeletionBlocked ResourceGroupDeletionTimeout

symptom: Cannot delete ACI subnet or VNet — error 'Subnet requires delegations [Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups] to reference service association link acisal' even after all ACI container groups are deleted
rootCause: Dangling ACI Service Association Link (acisal) remains on subnet after container groups are deleted. The acisal has a special flag allowing subnet deletion but cascaded delete from VNet/RG level may fail. Direct subnet deletion may succeed due to the special flag.
solution: 1) Try explicit subnet deletion from Portal or CLI (not cascaded from VNet/RG). 2) If that fails, validate all deployments are deleted using Kusto SubscriptionDeployments query. 3) Delete SAL directly: az resource delete --ids /subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/{vnet}/subnets/{subnet}/serviceAssociationLinks/acisal --api-version 2018-10-01. 4) If all fail, open IcM to ACIS for SAL deletion via ACIS action.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Cannot delete ACI Vnet or Subnet due to dangling Service Association Link

symptom: ACI deployment fails with 'ContainerGroups quota exceeded, Limit: 0, Usage: 0, Requested: 1' / ServiceUnavailable — subscription shows available quota but deployment is blocked
rootCause: ACI's internal fraud detection logic was triggered. Multiple ACI deployments in multiple regions using the same container group name at the same time can flag the subscription. The subscription or tenant gets added to the fraud-config.json blocklist, setting effective quota to 0.
solution: 1) Confirm fraud flag: check ACI RP traces with operationName contains 'Fraud' in Kusto (Accprod.accprod.Traces). 2) Verify tenant/subscription in fraud-config.json in Compute-ACI-Config repo. 3) Advise Cx to deploy one ACI at a time to avoid triggering fraud detection. 4) Create IcM copy of 394233833 for HIT (Human Intelligence Team) to validate no other fraudulent behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Fraud Detected

symptom: Cannot delete ACI VNet or Subnet — error 'Subnet requires delegations [Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups] to reference service association link .../serviceAssociationLinks/acisal' even after all ACI container groups have been removed
rootCause: ACI creates a Service Association Link (acisal) on the delegated subnet. After all container groups are deleted, the acisal may remain (dangling), blocking subnet/VNET deletion. Setting subnet delegation to 'None' does not work because the acisal is still present.
solution: 1) Verify all ACI deployments on the subnet are deleted using Kusto query on accprod.SubscriptionDeployments filtering by subnetId. 2) Delete the SAL via CLI: az resource delete --ids /subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/{vnet}/subnets/{subnet}/serviceAssociationLinks/acisal --api-version 2018-10-01. 3) Retry subnet deletion. 4) If still fails, open IcM to ACI team for ACIS Action NRP/DeleteServiceAssociationLink.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Cannot delete ACI Vnet or Subnet due to dangling Service Association Link

symptom: ACI deployment fails with 'container group quota ContainerGroups exceeded in region — Limit: 0, Usage: 0, Requested: 1' (ServiceUnavailable) even though subscription shows available quota
rootCause: ACI internal fraud detection (FraudDetected) was triggered for the subscription or tenant. This can happen when multiple ACI deployments are issued in multiple regions using the same name simultaneously (e.g., from automation scripts). The subscription/tenant is added to the fraud-config.json blocklist, setting quota limit to 0.
solution: 1) Confirm fraud detection via Kusto: query accprod.Traces where operationName contains 'Fraud' for the subscription. 2) Check if sub/tenant is in the fraud blocklist: https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_git/Compute-ACI-Config?path=/src/Fraud/fraud-config.json. 3) Advise customer to deploy 1 ACI request at a time to avoid re-triggering. 4) Create IcM copy of 394233833 for HIT (Human Intelligence Team) to validate no other fraudulent behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Fraud Detected

symptom: ACI MSI token request failure — AccessTokenRequestEvent shows successful=False; MSI-based authentication fails for container instances
rootCause: Subscription may lack access to the managed identity, or an ARM limitation/throttle exists on the subscription preventing token acquisition.
solution: 1) Query Kusto AccessTokenRequestEvent filtering by TIMESTAMP, Tenant (region), successful==False. 2) For BadRequest errors, verify subscription has access to the managed identity. 3) Check for ARM limitations using Kusto query on cluster armprodgbl against ARMProd.Unionizer filtering by subscriptionId and PUT method, joined with Traces containing 'Limitation'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] MSITokenRequestFailure

symptom: Cluster autoscaler deletes AKS nodes running batch Jobs, causing job interruption and data loss
rootCause: The safe-to-evict annotation is either not set, or is incorrectly placed in the Job spec instead of the Pod template spec. Without this annotation on the Pod, the cluster autoscaler considers the node eligible for scale-down even while a Job pod is running.
solution: Add annotation 'cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict: "false"' in the Pod template metadata (spec.template.metadata.annotations), NOT in the Job metadata. Verify with 'kubectl describe pod <pod-name>' to confirm the annotation is present on the pod. Reference: https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/FAQ.md#what-types-of-pods-can-prevent-ca-from-removing-a-node
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Cluster Management/Preventing cluster autoscaler from deleting a running job

symptom: ACI MSI token request failure — AccessTokenRequestEvent shows successful=False, may present as BadRequest or timeout when container group tries to acquire managed identity token
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: 1) Subscription does not have access to the managed identity resource. 2) ARM limitations applied to the subscription blocking PUT operations on identity resources. 3) Regional Tenant issues in WARP clusters.
solution: 1) Check Kusto AccessTokenRequestEvent table filtered by TIMESTAMP, Tenant/region, successful=False to identify failure details. 2) For BadRequest: verify subscription has proper RBAC access to the managed identity. 3) Check ARM limitations: query cluster('armprodgbl.eastus').database('ARMProd').Unionizer('Requests','HttpIncomingRequests') joined with Traces containing 'Limitation' to confirm if quota/policy limits are blocking identity operations.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] MSITokenRequestFailure

symptom: ACI Portal memory metrics show lower values than expected — running 'free -h' inside main container shows ~10 GiB usage but Portal displays ~5 GiB for a multi-container group
rootCause: Azure Portal memory metric for Container Groups is an AVERAGE aggregation across ALL containers in the group (not per-container). In a multi-container setup where main container uses 10 GiB and sidecar uses 0.02 GiB, the averaged metric shows ~5 GiB. Only average aggregation is available (Min/Max will not show data).
solution: 1) Explain to customer this is expected behavior — Portal metric averages across all containers. 2) Add a dimension filter in Azure Monitor to see metrics per individual container: go to Metrics → add filter → dimension = container name. 3) Reference docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-monitor#get-metrics---azure-portal and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/essentials/metrics-supported#microsoftcontainerinstancecontainergroups
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Memory usage inside container does not match values in Portal

symptom: AKS node sysctl configuration changes (e.g., net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal) do not persist after node reboot
rootCause: 'sysctl -w' only applies changes in-memory; without adding the nf_conntrack module to /etc/modules-load.d/modules.conf and creating a persistent config in /etc/sysctl.d/, changes are lost on reboot.
solution: 1) Add module: 'echo nf_conntrack >> /etc/modules-load.d/modules.conf'. 2) Create persistent config: 'echo "net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal=1" > /etc/sysctl.d/16-custom.conf'. 3) For automation, deploy a DaemonSet with privileged nsenter container that sets both module load and sysctl config persistently. 4) Verify after reboot with 'sysctl -a | grep nf_conntrack_tcp_be_liberal'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Compute/Linux/Making conntrack settings persistent in AKS Nodes

symptom: ACI memory usage in Azure Portal shows much lower values than 'free -h' inside the container — multi-container group memory metric mismatch
rootCause: Azure Portal memory metric for ACI shows the average across ALL containers in the Container Group (averaged over 1-minute granularity). In multi-container groups, this averages the main container's usage with sidecar containers that use minimal memory, resulting in misleadingly low values.
solution: This is expected behavior. Add a dimension filter in Portal Metrics to view per-container memory usage: go to Metrics → select Memory Usage → Add Filter → containerName. Note: only Average aggregation is available for ACI metrics (Min/Max show no data). Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-monitor#get-metrics---azure-portal
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Memory usage inside container does not match values in Portal

symptom: AKS nodepool SKU resize fails with error 'OS disk of Ephemeral VM with size greater than 100 GB is not allowed for VM size Standard_D4s_v3 when the DiffDiskPlacement is CacheDisk' — cannot change VM size when ephemeral OS disk is attached
rootCause: Cannot directly resize nodepool VM SKU when ephemeral OS disks are attached. The target SKU's cache disk size may not accommodate the current ephemeral disk size. Additionally, StatefulSets with Delete reclaim policy will lose PV data during migration.
solution: Step-by-step migration: 1) Create new nodepool with desired SKU (match AZ config). 2) Snapshot all PVs from Portal. 3) Cordon old nodepool nodes. 4) Restore PVCs from snapshots (match AZ). 5) Backup StatefulSet YAML. 6) Delete StatefulSet, PV, PVC. 7) Create new PV pointing to restored disk with matching labels. 8) Update PVC with matchLabels and StatefulSet with correct volume names. 9) Apply configs. 10) Drain and delete old nodepool.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Compute/Upgrading nodepool SKUs with ephemeral disks and stateful storage

symptom: Cannot collect container logs via kubectl logs when AKS tunnel/konnectivity is down — API server cannot reach kubelet
rootCause: When the AKS tunnel (konnectivity/SSH) connection is unavailable, kubectl logs cannot proxy through the API server to reach the kubelet on the node, making standard log collection impossible.
solution: Collect logs directly from the node's kubelet API: 1) Find node IP: 'kubectl get pod -owide' + 'kubectl get node -owide'. 2) Get bearer token from kubeconfig or ServiceAccount secret. 3) From a VM with network connectivity to node IP on TCP:10250, use: curl -k -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' https://<nodeIP>:10250/containerLogs/<namespace>/<podName>/<containerName>. Format: https://<nodeIP>:10250/containerLogs/<namespace>/<pod>/<container>.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Monitoring/Collect container logs directly from a node's kubelet (i.e. when tunnel is down)

symptom: ACI 部署失败，返回 ServiceUnavailable (409)：'The requested resource is not available in the location at this moment'，请求的 CPU/内存/OS/VNet 组合在目标区域不可用
rootCause: 目标区域不支持所请求的资源配置（CPU/Memory/OS 组合），或客户部署到了不可用的区域，或使用了该区域不支持的功能特性。
solution: 1) 查看 Resource availability by region 文档确认区域支持；2) 调用 Location List Capabilities API 验证可用特性；3) 检查请求中是否含不支持的功能；4) 使用 ARM Kusto 查询 (armprodgbl Unionizer + Traces where message contains 'Limitation') 按 correlationId 关联找出具体限制原因。
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Service Unavailable Error

symptom: Kubernetes system pod logs lost after master node reboot — kubectl logs only shows logs from after the most recent boot
rootCause: Kubelet spins up a new set of system pods after reboot. The new pods' logs start from the reboot time, so previous pod logs are no longer accessible via kubectl logs.
solution: Docker daemon preserves logs of previous container instances in 'Exited' status across reboots. 1) Find old containers: 'docker ps -a -f status=exited'. 2) Access logs at /var/lib/docker/containers/<ContainerID>/. 3) Collect all logs: 'tar -cvzf /tmp/kubesystemlogs.tgz /var/lib/docker/containers/*/*.log'. This includes pre-reboot logs for kube-apiserver, controller-manager, scheduler, etc.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Monitoring/Collect Kubernetes POD logs prior to last reboot

symptom: ACI 部署在某区域持续返回 ServiceUnavailable (409)，尽管该区域文档上列为可用；该区域多个客户同时受影响
rootCause: 区域内 K8s 集群处于不健康状态或 scheduleEnabled=false 导致集群被禁用，无可用容量。可能因 K8s Service unhealthy、证书过期等原因导致集群被标记为不可调度。
solution: 1) 使用 ClusterHealth Kusto 查询按 location 过滤，检查 scheduleEnabled、state、provisioningState 状态；2) 对 Atlas 区域检查 InventoryManager Jarvis Dashboard；3) 确认集群 API Server、MSI Connector、Controller Manager 等组件健康状态；4) 如容量不足需部署新集群扩容。
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Service Unavailable Error

symptom: ACI container logs not appearing in Log Analytics Workspace — ContainerInstanceLog_CL table missing; az container log works but LA Workspace shows no data
rootCause: Three possible causes: 1) Invalid or expired Log Analytics Workspace key preventing authentication. 2) Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS) configured with public network ingestion disabled — ACI log integration does not support AMPLS. 3) Local authentication disabled on LA Workspace (disableLocalAuth=true), preventing ACI from authenticating.
solution: 1) Verify LA Workspace key validity. 2) If AMPLS is used, enable data ingestion from public network: set 'Accept data ingestion from public networks' to Yes. 3) Check local auth: 'az resource show --ids /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws>' — if disableLocalAuth=true, enable it: 'az resource update --ids <ws-id> --api-version 2021-06-01 --set properties.features.disableLocalAuth=false'. Diagnostic: query SbzExecLogUploaderEvent in Kusto — look for 'Successfully posted' (success) or 'Upload failed with Http 403' (failure).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Unable to send ACI logs to Log Analytics Workspace

symptom: ACR image tags/repositories disappearing unexpectedly — pulls fail with 'manifest unknown' or 'tag not found'; unclear whether caused by retention policy or external automation
rootCause: External automation (CI/CD pipeline, script, scheduled job) is performing tag deletions. ACR retention policy only deletes untagged manifests after configured days. Delete events with Tag populated (WithTag > 0) indicate external tag untagging, not retention. Once tags are removed, manifests become untagged and retention may later purge them.
solution: Investigate via Kusto RegistryManifestEvent queries: 1) Confirm delete activity volume (summarize by Action). 2) Check cadence — bursty spikes suggest automation. 3) Determine if Delete is tag-deletion vs manifest-delete: summarize WithTag=countif(isnotempty(Tag)), WithoutTag=countif(isempty(Tag)). 4) Check patterns across repos — similar counts suggest rule-based cleanup. 5) Check timing distribution (bin by 5m). 6) List top deleted tags to identify CI/CD patterns. Key: WithTag>0 = external automation; mostly PurgeManifest = retention.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image deletion investigation

symptom: ACI deployment fails with 'ServiceUnavailable (409)' — 'The requested resource is not available in the location at this moment. Please retry with a different resource request or in another location'
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: 1) Customer deploying to region/feature combination not yet available (check region availability). 2) ARM limitations blocking the subscription. 3) Regional capacity exhaustion — clusters disabled or at max capacity. 4) For K8s clusters: unhealthy services (API server, controller manager, etc.) causing scheduleEnabled=false.
solution: 1) Verify region availability at https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-region-availability. 2) Check feature availability via REST API ListCapabilities. 3) Check for ARM limitations: query armprodgbl Unionizer for 409 status with 'Limitation' in Traces. 4) For Atlas regions: check InventoryManager Jarvis dashboard. 5) For K8s regions: query ClusterHealth for capacity (availableReplicas, scheduleEnabled, service health). 6) Add capacity if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACI/TSG/[TSG] Service Unavailable Error

symptom: AKS nodepool scaling fails — 'Load balancer profile allocated ports is not in an allowable range given the number of nodes and IPs provisioned' — max surge nodes exceed available load balancer ports
rootCause: Max surge value (percentage or fixed) applied to max node count causes total required ports to exceed available ports from load balancer public IPs. Each IP provides 64,000 ephemeral ports; required ports = (max_nodes + surge_nodes) * ports_per_node. When this exceeds available_IPs * 64,000, scaling fails.
solution: 1) Check node pool setup in ASC for ports/node and surge values. 2) Calculate max supported nodes: (num_IPs * 64000) / ports_per_node. 3) Ensure active_nodes + surge_nodes never exceed this max. 4) Options to fix: reduce surge %, add more public IPs to LB, decrease ports_per_node (must be multiple of 8), or adjust maxCount. Example: 2560 ports/node with 2 IPs = max 50 nodes (128000/2560). Surge of 33% on maxCount=10 needs 13 nodes total.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/How Tos/Cluster Management/Scaling_in_Azure_Kubernetes_Service_(AKS)_with_Surge_Setup_based_on_the_Loadbalancer_IP_count

symptom: Customer requests proactive or standby support for AKS cluster upgrade, certificate rotation, or other planned maintenance operations
rootCause: AKS does not support proactive/standby scenarios through Microsoft Support. This is by design per AKS support policy.
solution: Inform customer that proactive/standby support is not available for AKS. For eligible customers, direct them to Azure Event Management (AEM) paid service which must be set up in advance. Customer should raise a case of correct severity if they encounter an issue during operations. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/support-policies
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/Additional Case Handling Processes/Break and Fix definition

symptom: After upgrading AKS cluster (e.g. 1.18.14 -> 1.26.10 -> 1.28.5), Istio operator pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff, Istio ingress/egress not working, applications fail to deploy
rootCause: Older version of Istio Helm chart incompatible with the upgraded AKS/Kubernetes version
solution: Upgrade Istio to a version compatible with the current AKS version. Check Istio pod logs (kubectl logs) for specific errors. Note: Istio is a third-party application outside Azure support scope; direct customer to Istio community for in-depth Istio-specific support
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/On Going Cases/Best Practices For Customer Calls/Sample Scenario For Setting Right Expectations

symptom: Customer reports AKS cluster in failed state; ASC shows (deleted) next to cluster name, indicating the cluster was deleted before support investigation
rootCause: Customer deleted the cluster before support could investigate the reported issue
solution: Check AppLens for pre-deletion logs. Inform customer cluster was deleted and cannot be recovered. If issue recurs with new cluster, request: cluster name, resource group, subscription ID, reproduction steps (Portal/CLI/PowerShell/CloudShell), error details, screenshots, and business impact.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/On Going Cases/Example Scenario Based Responses/Customer Deletes Cluster

symptom: Customer running deprecated/unsupported AKS version (outside N-2 full support or N-3 platform support) encounters issues such as Windows node deadlock or other known bugs fixed in newer versions
rootCause: Cluster is on an unsupported Kubernetes version; known bugs (e.g., containerd deadlock in v1.26) are fixed in newer AKS releases but not backported to EOL versions
solution: Attempt FQR and basic troubleshooting first; if issue is fixed in newer version, ask customer to upgrade using az aks upgrade --name <cluster> --resource-group <rg> --kubernetes-version <version>; check supported versions with az aks get-versions --location <region> --output table; provide AKS version support policy link; offer alternatives like porting to Linux nodepool if upgrade not immediately actionable
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/On Going Cases/Example Scenario Based Responses/Unsupported Version of AKS

symptom: CustomerNotifyWait error in Jarvis Actions when attempting AKS LockBox CustomerData access; JIT request alone insufficient to unlock live AKS object logs
rootCause: Customer subscription has Customer Lockbox for Microsoft Azure enabled, requiring explicit customer consent before Microsoft engineers can access customer data
solution: Contact customer to approve the pending Lockbox request via Azure Portal > Customer Lockbox > Pending Requests. If customer declines consent, troubleshoot via screensharing or ask customer to attach needed information to the support request. Note: only Global admins can enable/disable Customer Lockbox; request expires after 4 days; access granted for 8 hours default.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Case Management/Customer Lockbox for Microsoft Azure - (JIT RequestApproval + Cx Approval) required

symptom: Engineer cannot see customer data in ASC (Azure Support Center), message shows 'This Section may contain sensitive data'
rootCause: Customer declined to provide consent for advanced diagnostic data collection when creating the support case
solution: 1) Click 'Learn More' in ASC to get email template, ask customer to provide consent via Azure Portal > Support Request > Allow collection of advanced diagnostic information; 2) If customer refuses consent, troubleshoot via screensharing or ask customer to attach needed info
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Case Management/Protecting Customer Data in ASC Logs Access Consent

symptom: JIT access request rejected when requesting AKS JIT via aka.ms/aksjit on SAW machine for Jarvis/Geneva Actions
rootCause: Engineer is not a member of required OneIdentity groups AME\TM-AZCTS-AKS and AME\TM-AZCTS, or not a member of CoreIdentity entitlement WA CTS -14817
solution: 1) Join CoreIdentity entitlement WA CTS-14817 at https://coreidentity.microsoft.com (Corp-joined + Azure VPN). 2) Join OneIdentity groups AME\TM-AZCTS-AKS and AME\TM-AZCTS at https://aka.ms/oneidentity (SAW only). 3) Verify claims at https://tokendiagnosticprod.azurewebsites.net/ - check for Group claim REDMOND\RRD-WACTSRG-16492. 4) If still failing, contact jitsupport@ or file IcM to Azure Security Engineering JIT (ServiceId 10042, TeamId 34736)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Internal Operations/Requesting JIT

symptom: After Kubernetes version upgrade, customer cannot connect to application through nginx ingress controller. Ingress stops routing traffic post-upgrade.
rootCause: Kubernetes version upgrade breaks compatibility with nginx ingress controller (and other cluster add-ons like LinkerD, akv2k8s). Nginx ingress has a supported versions matrix that must be matched to the K8s version.
solution: Check nginx ingress supported versions table (https://github.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx#supported-versions-table) and upgrade nginx ingress to a version compatible with the new K8s version. Similarly check compatibility for all other workloads/add-ons deployed on the cluster.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Strategic Customer Processes/AT&T Reminders and Guidelines

symptom: AKS cluster cannot add new nodes after user modified VMSS extensions. Node provisioning fails because required components are missing from the VM.
rootCause: User changed the VMSS extension at VM level, which is not supported per Azure documentation. Custom VM extensions on AKS node pools can break required node components.
solution: Use AKSNodeInstaller DaemonSet to reinstall required components on affected nodes. Project: https://github.com/patnaikshekhar/AKSNodeInstaller. Note: nsenter behavior changed in Ubuntu 18.04 - update runOnHost.sh from /usr/bin/nsenter -m/proc/1/ns/mnt to /usr/bin/nsenter -n -m/proc/1/ns/mnt.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/AKS Cheatsheet

symptom: After stopping and restarting a private AKS cluster via Azure Automation runbook, the cluster becomes inaccessible due to broken private DNS zone link to the virtual network
rootCause: Azure Automation Account configured at subscription level stops all AKS clusters including private ones. Stopping a private AKS cluster can break the private DNS zone link to the virtual network, causing DNS resolution failures for the API server FQDN
solution: Exclude private AKS clusters from the automation schedule by adding filtering logic in the runbook. If already affected, re-link the private DNS zone to the virtual network per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/private-clusters
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/Miscellaneous/Resource Shutdown Automation

symptom: When trying to send or reply to email from DFM (Dynamics 365), receive Access Is Denied error (Error code: 0x80048306). The error prevents outgoing emails from being sent.
rootCause: DFM email permission issue when replying directly with existing recipients in To/CC fields.
solution: Before clicking Send, copy all email addresses from the To and CC sections, delete them, then re-add them. This clears the permission error and allows the email to be sent.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/Support/DfM and Case Buddy/Workaround for DFM outgoing email Access is Denied error

symptom: X509 certificate authentication error on SAW machine when using AME card - incorrect cert path prevents trust chain creation
rootCause: The AME certificate has an incorrect path that does not create the trust chain. The intermediate CA certificate (AME SC CA 01) is missing from the local certificate store.
solution: Download and install the certificate chain: open cert > Details > Authority Information Access > copy URL > download and install. Or run PowerShell (non-admin): curl "http://crl.microsoft.com/pkiinfra/Certs/BY2PKISCCA01.AME.GBL_AME SC CA 01(3).crt" -OutFile c:\windows	emp y2SC.cer; Import-Certificate -CertStoreLocation cert:\CurrentUser\CA\ -FilePath c:\windows	emp y2SC.cer
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/Support/SAW and CorpVM/AME Account Access Overview/Common Issues/AME Error when using X509 cert authentication on SAW

symptom: Azure Network Policy Manager (NPM) is being deprecated. NPM has scaling limitations (max 250 nodes), security gaps (cannot enforce NetworkPolicies on LoadBalancer/NodePort with externalTrafficPolicy=Cluster), and lacks FQDN/L7/cluster-wide policy features.
rootCause: NPM has fundamental scaling, security, and feature limitations. Upstream is transitioning to Cilium as default CNI for AKS.
solution: For Linux nodes: migrate to Cilium Network Policies (scales to 3000+ nodes). Use migration guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/migrate-from-npm-to-cilium-network-policy. For Windows nodes: switch to Calico or use NSGs. Linux NPM support ends Sept 2028, Windows NPM support ends Sept 2026.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/AKS Network Policy

symptom: Customer attempts to remove server response headers using Envoy Lua filters in Azure Managed Istio, but the Server header is re-inserted by Envoy after Lua filter execution. Azure Managed Istio blocks advanced EnvoyFilter patches (e.g., ROUTE_CONFIGURATION).
rootCause: Envoy adds the Server header after Lua filters execute. Azure Managed Istio denylists EnvoyFilter and WasmPlugin resources, preventing customers from applying advanced configurations to strip the header.
solution: Create an IcM with PG to request whitelisting of envoy filters for the specific user subscription. Upon approval, PG can activate the Envoy filter toggle for the AKS cluster. Note: Envoy filters in Azure Managed Istio are not GA or officially supported; support is best-effort only.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Addons and Extensions/Envoy_filters_Constraints_in_Azure_managed_Istio

symptom: AKS operations fail with 'failed calling webhook' errors; upgrade operations show 'broken pipe' in API server logs when calling kyverno or other webhook endpoints; pod creation/scaling fails with 'context deadline exceeded' on webhook admission endpoints
rootCause: Connectivity between API server instances and webhook handler pods is disrupted; konnectivity-agent tunnel is unhealthy or webhook pods are unavailable to handle incoming admission requests
solution: Immediate mitigation: kubectl delete validatingwebhookconfiguration <webhook-name> or kubectl delete mutatingwebhookconfiguration <webhook-name>. Then investigate konnectivity-agent tunnel health and check webhook pod status for readiness
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/Cluster Management/Kubernetes admission webhook related TSG

symptom: CVM enabled node pools cannot be created with Arm64, Trusted Launch, FIPS, or Pod Sandboxing settings enabled
rootCause: Confidential VM (CVM) node pools have specific hardware/firmware requirements that are incompatible with Arm64 architecture, Trusted Launch, FIPS, and Pod Sandboxing features
solution: Remove the incompatible setting (Arm64/Trusted Launch/FIPS/Pod Sandboxing) before creating the CVM node pool. Use only supported AMD SEV-SNP compatible VM SKUs (DCav5/ECav5)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/AKS Confidential VM (CVM)

symptom: KMS etcd encryption fails with StatusCode=403 ForbiddenByFirewall: 'Client address is not authorized and caller is not a trusted service' when KMS plugin tries to encrypt/decrypt secrets via Azure Key Vault
rootCause: Azure Key Vault has firewall enabled, blocking the KMS plugin (running on AKS control plane) from accessing the vault endpoint
solution: Disable the firewall on Azure Key Vault to allow KMS plugin access. Alternatively, if using private key vault, enable API Server VNet Integration (az aks update --enable-apiserver-vnet-integration) and add the API server subnet to Key Vault allowed networks
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/KMS etcd Encryption

symptom: AKS LocalDNS validation failure when configuring Root zone (.) with forwardDestination set to ClusterCoreDNS under vnetDNSOverrides. API returns validation error rejecting the configuration.
rootCause: By design, the Root zone under vnetDNSOverrides cannot use ClusterCoreDNS as forwardDestination. This would create a routing loop since VnetDNS traffic for the root zone must go to the actual VNET DNS servers, not back to cluster CoreDNS.
solution: Set forwardDestination to VnetDNS for the Root zone under vnetDNSOverrides. Only cluster.local zone can use ClusterCoreDNS as the forward destination in vnetDNSOverrides.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/LocalDNS

symptom: Customer reports Azure Disk CSI Driver issues but cluster uses the upstream OSS version instead of AKS-managed version. Pods fail to mount/attach Azure Disks with CSI driver errors.
rootCause: AKS only supports the managed version of Azure Disk CSI Driver v2. The upstream open-source version is not supported by Microsoft and may have compatibility issues with AKS infrastructure.
solution: 1) Check ASI (https://azureserviceinsights.trafficmanager.net/) to verify if managed version is enabled (blue check mark in Feature section); 2) If using OSS version, direct customer to upstream TSG and GitHub issues; 3) For managed version issues, classify by type: Creation/Deletion (check csi-azuredisk-controller in CCP), Attach/Detach (check controller + VM nodes), Mount/Unmount (check csi-azuredisk-node on customer nodes + kubelet logs).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Preview Features/Azure Disk CSI Driver v2

symptom: Customer requests troubleshooting help for third-party software, OSS (e.g. Dremio, non-addon Istio, Nginx Ingress Controller), customer application code, or unsupported services (AKS Engine, vanilla k8s, on-prem k8s) running on AKS
rootCause: Third-party software, OSS, customer application code, and unsupported services are outside AKS support scope. Microsoft provides best-effort support only when third-party OSS integrates with Azure cloud provider.
solution: First verify AKS platform components are healthy. For supported integrations with Azure cloud provider, provide best-effort support using Microsoft documentation examples. For pure third-party/OSS issues, explain support boundaries and direct customer to vendor. Best-effort rules: single engineer/region, no 24x7/FTS/handovers, no guarantee of resolution. References: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/support-policies and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/azure/general/support-policy-containers
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/Additional Case Handling Processes/Break and Fix definition

symptom: Customer requests information about which user performed a specific operation such as deleting an Azure resource or Kubernetes component
solution: Customer own Support Data can be shared per Risk Management guidelines. Direct to Activity Logs (Azure Portal) for ARM operations or kube-audit logs (Log Analytics) for K8s operations. Refs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/monitor-aks-reference#activity-log, https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/monitor-aks#sample-log-queries
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/On Going Cases/Example Scenario Based Responses/Request for Actions on Customer Side

symptom: DfM fails to retrieve entitlements for TPID; case severity defaults to C and cannot be changed; error banner displayed when attempting severity change
rootCause: DfM entitlement retrieval failure for the case, potentially due to missing or incorrect entitlement association
solution: Copy subscription ID from case details > click dropdown arrow next to Edit SAP > select Edit Entitlement > paste subscription ID and search > select appropriate entitlement from results > Save
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Case Management/Fix Incorrect Case Entitlement

symptom: Case created outside Azure Portal (phone, Partner portal, Dynamics), customer data not visible in ASC
rootCause: Cases created outside the Azure Portal do not trigger the data consent workflow, so customer data is not available in ASC
solution: 1) Troubleshoot manually using screensharing or file attachments; 2) Ask customer to create a new support request in Azure Portal using the email template from 'Learn More' link
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Case Management/Protecting Customer Data in ASC Logs Access Consent

symptom: JIT Get Access window remains stuck in processing state when trying to access different Jarvis operations for AKS troubleshooting
rootCause: Known issue with JIT Get Access UI when switching between different Jarvis operations after JIT approval
solution: Workaround TBD (known issue). If JIT was approved but Get Access still fails, contact jitsupport@ or file IcM to Azure Security Engineering JIT on-call (ServiceId 10042, TeamId 34736). Note: only submit Get Access request once as it requires manual approval.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Internal Operations/Requesting JIT

symptom: Intermittent networking issues in AKS pods that are difficult to reproduce. Need continuous packet capture without filling OS disk.
solution: Deploy k8s-tcp-dump-sidecar as a sidecar container for 24-hour rolling TCP dump traces, sending captures to Azure Files. This avoids filling the OS disk with continuous captures. Project: https://github.com/gjlumsden/k8s-tcp-dump-sidecar
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/AKS Cheatsheet

symptom: JIT access request immediately rejected: "The default JIT policy has been applied, request rejected as no elevation policy is in place"
rootCause: AME.gbl account is not assigned to the AME\TM-AZCTS-AKS security group, which became mandatory since July 15, 2020 for elevated JIT access.
solution: Join TM-AZCTS-AKS security group: SAW > Edge > https://oneidentity.core.windows.net > sign in with AME cert > Create Request > Security Group > domain=ame > search TM-AZCTS-AKS > add alias. Verify at https://jitaccess.security.core.windows.net/AuthInfo.aspx. If group already present, open ticket at aka.ms/cdocidmrequest.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/Support/SAW and CorpVM/AME Account Access Overview/Common Issues/Why was my JIT access request rejected

symptom: KEDA operator logs show 'Failed to get API Group-Resources' with error 'Get https://10.0.0.1:443/api?timeout=32s: EOF'. KEDA metric server shows 'unable to run external metrics adapter' with same EOF error. KEDA pods fail to start.
rootCause: KEDA add-on cannot reach the Kubernetes API server due to misconfigured cluster firewall blocking required outbound network traffic.
solution: Configure the cluster firewall to allow required outbound traffic per AKS egress requirements: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/outbound-rules-control-egress
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Addons and Extensions/KEDA

symptom: InvalidOSSKU error when creating CVM node pool: OSSKU=Ubuntu2204 is invalid, osSKU Ubuntu2204 and Ubuntu2404 are not supported on CVM VM sizes
rootCause: Only specific OS versions support CVM: Ubuntu 20.04 (k8s 1.24-1.34), Ubuntu 24.04 (k8s 1.32-1.38), Azure Linux 3.0 (k8s 1.31+). Ubuntu 22.04, Azure Linux 2.0, and Windows are not supported
solution: Use a supported OS SKU for CVM node pools: Ubuntu2004 (k8s 1.24-1.34), Ubuntu2404 (k8s 1.32+), or AzureLinux3 (k8s 1.31+). Refer to the CVM supported OS versions compatibility table
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/AKS Confidential VM (CVM)

symptom: KMS etcd encryption fails with StatusCode=403 ForbiddenByPolicy: AKS managed identity does not have keys encrypt permission on key vault
rootCause: Key Vault access policy is missing Encrypt and Decrypt key permissions for the AKS cluster managed identity (or the permissions were deleted)
solution: Assign Key Vault access policy with Encrypt and Decrypt key permissions for the AKS managed identity. Follow https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/assign-access-policy
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/KMS etcd Encryption

symptom: AKS LocalDNS validation failure when configuring cluster.local zone with forwardDestination set to VnetDNS under vnetDNSOverrides or kubeDNSOverrides. API rejects the configuration.
rootCause: By design, the cluster.local zone cannot forward to VnetDNS. Kubernetes internal service names (cluster.local) must be resolved by CoreDNS within the cluster, not by external VNET DNS servers.
solution: Set forwardDestination to ClusterCoreDNS for the cluster.local zone in both vnetDNSOverrides and kubeDNSOverrides.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Feature Specific/LocalDNS

symptom: Validation errors when creating a Managed Cluster Snapshot: OperationNotSupportedAPIVersion, FailedToDeserializeRequestBody, SubscriptionNotFound, SubscriptionStateInvalid, ManagedClusterSnapshotPreviewFeatureNotRegistered, MaxSnapshotCountExceeded, SnapshotBodyWithoutSourceResourceId, SourceResourceIDNotExistsOrNotInSameRegion, or SourceManagedClusterNotHaveRequiredProperties.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: API version too old (need >=v20220202preview), wrong request body format, subscription not found or invalid state, preview feature not registered, snapshot count exceeds limit (default 100), missing cluster-id in body, source cluster deleted or not in same region, or source cluster is in broken/non-Succeeded state.
solution: 1) Use API version >=v20220202preview; 2) Verify request body format matches spec; 3) Check subscription exists and is in valid state; 4) Register ManagedClusterSnapshotPreviewFeature; 5) Check snapshot count vs limit (default 100); 6) Include source cluster-id in request body; 7) Verify source cluster exists in same region and is in Succeeded state. Use Kusto: AKSprod.FrontEndContextActivity with operationName=PutManagedClusterSnapshotHandler.PUT.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/Platform and Tools/Preview Features/Managed Cluster Snapshot

symptom: Customer requests Root Cause Analysis (RCA) for a past AKS incident or outage, especially when node or logs have been deleted
rootCause: RCA is performed on best-efforts basis without SLA guarantees. If nodes/logs were deleted, investigation capability is severely limited.
solution: Handle RCA requests at Severity B/C (not Sev A). Do not hand over across regions except in rare political situations. Set clear expectation that RCA is best-effort with no guarantee. If logs are unavailable, explain that investigation is limited by available data. Use professional tone when communicating limitations.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Case Handling E2E/Additional Case Handling Processes/Break and Fix definition

symptom: Customer submits AKS support case for Deployments, Namespaces, containerizing applications, or complex advisory scenarios
rootCause: These scenarios are outside AKS support scope. AKS support covers: API server connectivity, VM registration, horizontal scaling, version upgrades only
solution: Inform customer that AKS support scope covers: 1) API server connectivity; 2) VM cluster registration; 3) Horizontal scaling; 4) Minor/patch version upgrades. For Deployments/Namespaces/containerizing/advisory, refer to Kubernetes community. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-US/troubleshoot/azure/general/support-policy-containers
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Case Management/Support Scope

symptom: After Microsoft alias change, engineer loses access to previously handled support cases in ASC (Azure Support Center) and related systems (DfM, VDM, WfM)
rootCause: ASC and related systems do not automatically update access permissions after alias changes; known system limitation requiring manual intervention
solution: 1) ASC cases: assign case to a peer then re-assign back to new alias. 2) Re-assign Yubikey at https://cloudmfa.support.windows.net/SecurityKeyServices/AssignSecurityKey and re-setup. 3) Rejoin CloudEnterprise and CloudEnterpriseAME silos. 4) Have direct manager update DfM, VDM, and WfM access for new alias.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Processes/Internal Operations/Post Alias Change Actions

symptom: Need to capture network traces across multiple AKS Linux nodes simultaneously when application has many replicas spread across nodes.
solution: Use dspcap tool to capture network traces on all Linux nodes at once. Project: https://github.com/tdihp/dspcap. Useful when customer application has many replicas on multiple nodes and individual node captures are impractical.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACT Team/Tools/AKS Cheatsheet

symptom: NGINX Ingress controller (App Routing add-on) is being retired. Upstream Kubernetes deprecated NGINX ingress controller project with retirement date March 2026.
rootCause: Upstream Kubernetes NGINX ingress controller project end-of-life. No more security or bug fixes from upstream.
solution: Microsoft supports NGINX App Routing with critical security fixes until November 2026.
