symptom: azure-defender-publisher DaemonSet pods continually fail and restart after enabling Defender Profile for AKS via Azure Advisor recommendation
rootCause: Authentication issues or misconfiguration when Defender Profile is enabled alongside Azure Policy Add-on for AKS.
solution: Workaround: Disable Azure Defender profile via REST API PUT call setting securityProfile.azureDefender.enabled=false. Use az account get-access-token to get bearer token, then curl PUT to management.azure.com.

symptom: Istio CNI enabled but istio-init containers still injected into new pods, CNI not performing traffic redirection
rootCause: Istio CNI DaemonSet not properly provisioned - CNI plugin installation failures, network config errors, or node taints preventing CNI pod scheduling
solution: Verify CNI DaemonSet running on all nodes: kubectl get daemonset azure-service-mesh-istio-cni-addon-node -n aks-istio-system; check DaemonSet pod logs for errors; verify node taints/tolerations allow CNI pod scheduling

symptom: Istio CNI enabled but pods fail to start with connection refused error in istio-validation init container
rootCause: CNI traffic redirection setup failed - istio-validation container detects that iptables rules were not properly configured by CNI plugin
solution: Check istio-validation logs: kubectl logs POD -c istio-validation; verify CNI DaemonSet is healthy; check pod events for network setup failures

symptom: Istio add-on egress gateway cannot be used on cluster
rootCause: Static Egress Gateway is not supported on Azure CNI Pod Subnet clusters
solution: Use a different CNI configuration (not Azure CNI Pod Subnet); verify cluster CNI plugin type before enabling Istio egress gateway

symptom: Istio egress gateway pods stuck in ContainerCreating state
rootCause: StaticGatewayConfiguration does not have egressIpPrefix assigned yet; kube-egress-gateway-cni-manager blocks istio-proxy creation until IP prefix provisioned (up to 5 min)
solution: Check StaticGatewayConfiguration status for egressIpPrefix; wait up to 5 min; verify gatewayNodepoolName references valid Gateway mode agent pool; check kube-egress-gateway-cni-manager logs

symptom: Istio Gateway API ingress not receiving traffic - Azure Load Balancer health probes failing
rootCause: Default Azure LB health probes not configured for Istio Gateway API deployment; health probe path/port/protocol mismatch
solution: Add Azure LB annotations to Gateway spec: health-probe-request-path=/healthz/ready, probe_protocol=http, probe_port=15021; or customize via GatewayClass/Gateway ConfigMap

symptom: High memory consumption in Envoy sidecar proxies across Istio mesh
rootCause: Envoy statistics collection with high cardinality metrics; Istio adds all cluster services to every Envoy config by default
solution: Use Sidecar CRD to restrict egress scope; enable discoverySelectors in MeshConfig; review and reduce custom metrics cardinality

symptom: Application pod becomes unresponsive or restarts when Istio sidecar is injected - race condition
rootCause: Application container starts before Envoy sidecar proxy is ready, causing network requests to fail
solution: Set holdApplicationUntilProxyStarts: true in MeshConfig defaultConfig section

symptom: Istio add-on error: Istio based Azure service mesh is incompatible with feature
rootCause: Conflicting extension (e.g. Open Service Mesh) enabled alongside Istio add-on
solution: Disable the conflicting feature and clean up corresponding resources before enabling Istio add-on

symptom: Istio ingress gateway pod crashes or not ready
rootCause: Istiod control plane not ready (ingress gateway depends on istiod); or gateway selector not matching correct label
solution: Verify istiod pod ready first; set correct gateway selector: istio: aks-istio-ingressgateway-external or internal; check Helm releases in aks-istio-ingress namespace

symptom: Istio secure ingress gateway TLS not working - certificate secret not created
rootCause: Azure Key Vault access for managed identity of secrets provider add-on set up incorrectly; SecretProviderClass or sync pod failed
solution: Verify secrets in aks-istio-ingress namespace; check Key Vault access policy for managed identity; verify SecretProviderClass and secrets-store-sync pod

symptom: Istio MeshConfig changes not taking effect or rejected
rootCause: Editing default ConfigMap instead of shared ConfigMap; tab characters in YAML; fields not on allowlist or misspelled; wrong revision in ConfigMap name
solution: Edit istio-shared-configmap-<revision> not istio-<revision>; use spaces not tabs; verify field names match allowlist; ensure ConfigMap name matches current revision

symptom: Istio Envoy using all CPU cores - high CPU consumption
rootCause: MeshConfig concurrency field set to 0, causing Envoy to use all available CPU cores
solution: Remove concurrency from MeshConfig; if not configured, CPU requests/limits determine core count

symptom: Data plane workloads dropped from Istio mesh after minor revision upgrade - sidecars no longer injected
rootCause: Namespace revision labels not updated to match new control plane revision before completing/rolling back upgrade
solution: Relabel namespaces: kubectl label namespace <ns> istio.io/rev=asm-x-y --overwrite; restart deployments to trigger sidecar reinjection; verify sidecar images

symptom: az aks mesh get-upgrades returns no available Istio upgrades
rootCause: Next Istio revision incompatible with current AKS cluster Kubernetes version
solution: Run az aks mesh get-revisions to check compatibility; upgrade cluster version first if needed; cluster upgrade permitted even if incompatible with current mesh

symptom: Istio plug-in CA - istiod pods stuck in Init:0/2 state with 403 Forbidden error
rootCause: Azure Key Vault access incorrectly configured - managed identity missing get/list permissions on Key Vault secrets
solution: Grant permissions: az keyvault set-policy --object-id <MI-objectId> --secret-permissions get list; for RBAC model add Key Vault Reader role; then reinstall Istio add-on

symptom: Istio plug-in CA - istiod pods stuck in Init:0/2 with SecretNotFound 404 error mounting cacerts volume
rootCause: Certificate secret objects missing or misconfigured in Azure Key Vault
solution: Verify all 4 secret objects exist in Key Vault with correct names; ensure objects are of type Secret (not Certificate or Key)

symptom: Istio plug-in CA certificate changes in Key Vault not auto-detected by cluster
rootCause: Auto-rotation for Azure Key Vault secrets provider add-on not enabled
solution: Enable auto-rotation; check with az aks show --query addonProfiles.azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider.config.enableSecretRotation; default poll interval 2 min

symptom: Pods with system-cluster-critical or system-node-critical priority class names are unexpectedly accepted and created in user namespaces in AKS
rootCause: Expected behavior by design; system-critical priority classes already exist in the system and are allowed in user namespaces
solution: No action required; this is intentional and not a security risk. Review other cluster security aspects if concerned

symptom: Azure Policy failure alert shows empty list of affected resources in AKS cluster
rootCause: Delay or Graph API issue causing alert to display empty resource list even if violation is resolved
solution: Wait for system to auto-update alert status; if persists >1 week, open Azure Support case with policy and alert details

symptom: Azure Policy cannot exclude privileged containers from AKS clusters
rootCause: Azure built-in policies lack container exclusion parameters for AKS clusters
solution: Create an exemption in default Azure Security Center policy, or create a new policy assignment that excludes desired pods/containers

symptom: Third-party scanner (e.g. Prisma) detects run-time host incident alert in AKS
rootCause: Alert typically generated by the container file system, not necessarily a real threat
solution: Review the specific container causing the alert; verify container and components come from trusted sources; ensure no unauthorized access indicators

symptom: KAITO workspace not created after enabling AI Toolchain Operator add-on; validation webhook blocks CR
rootCause: Wrong values in KAITO workspace custom resource specification rejected by workspace controller validation webhook
solution: Check gpu-provisioner and workspace pod logs; ensure GPU VM size meets model requirements; track with kubectl get machine/workspace -o wide

symptom: GPU node not created by KAITO gpu-provisioner controller; machine CR shows quota error
rootCause: GPU quota limitations in subscription or region prevent node provisioning
solution: Request quota increase for GPU VM family via Azure portal; check GPU instance availability in region; consider switching region or GPU VM size

symptom: KAITO model inference image not pulled; resource ready condition not True after long wait
rootCause: Inference images are very large (30-100GB); private image access mode may block pull; networking setup affects pull speed
solution: Verify image access mode and pull secrets; expect longer pull times for large images (tens of minutes); check cluster networking configuration

symptom: Azure App Configuration extension installation fails with Resource already existing in cluster / helm ownership metadata error
rootCause: Azure App Configuration Kubernetes Provider already installed via helm install; CRD ownership metadata conflicts with extension installation
solution: Uninstall Azure App Configuration Kubernetes Provider first (clean up resources), then install the AKS extension

symptom: Azure App Configuration extension installation fails with Failed to resolve the extension version
rootCause: Specified extension version does not exist or is not a supported version
solution: Retry installation using a supported version of the Azure App Configuration extension per official docs

symptom: Azure App Configuration extension fails with Extension type microsoft.appconfiguration is not registered in region
rootCause: Azure App Configuration extension not available in the specified AKS region
solution: Install in a supported region that the cluster extension supports

symptom: Dapr extension installation fails with no visible error message; extension status shows InstallationFailed
rootCause: Wrong key used in configuration settings (e.g., global.ha=false instead of global.ha.enabled=false), or extension-immutable-values configMap not found
solution: Inspect error via az k8s-extension list --cluster-type managedClusters; fix config key names; restart cluster; register KubernetesConfiguration service provider; or force delete and reinstall Dapr extension

symptom: Dapr extension install fails: ExtensionOperationFailed - Failed to resolve the extension version from the given values
rootCause: Targeted Dapr version does not exist
solution: Verify and use a supported Dapr version from the official supported versions list

symptom: Dapr extension install fails: ExtensionTypeRegistrationGetFailed - Extension type microsoft.dapr is not registered in region
rootCause: Dapr version not available in the specified Azure region
solution: Install Dapr extension in a region where the target version is supported

symptom: Dapr extension install fails: ServiceAccount dapr-operator in namespace dapr-system exists and cannot be imported - invalid ownership metadata
rootCause: Dapr OSS (open-source) already installed on cluster, conflicts with AKS Dapr extension Helm release
solution: Uninstall Dapr OSS first, then install the Dapr extension. See Migrate from Dapr OSS to Dapr extension guide

symptom: Dapr placement server pod unschedulable: 0/N nodes available, volume node affinity conflict
rootCause: Placement server pod tries to use persistent volume created in a different availability zone
solution: Install Dapr in multiple AZs (recommended), or limit placement service to specific AZ by creating custom StorageClass with allowedTopologies and setting dapr_placement.volumeclaims.storageClassName

symptom: AKS upgrade fails: UpgradeFailed - Evict blocked by conflicting disruption budgets; pod has more than one PodDisruptionBudget
rootCause: Pod covered by multiple PDBs; Kubernetes eviction API does not support evicting pods matched by more than one PDB, blocking node drain
solution: Identify conflicting PDBs via kubectl get pdb -n <ns> -o wide; delete one conflicting PDB so each pod is covered by at most one; reconcile and retry upgrade. Consider using Eviction Autoscaler extension for automatic PDB management

symptom: Kubernetes marketplace offer deployment fails: AuthorizationFailed - does not have authorization to perform extensionaddons/read
rootCause: Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration resource provider not registered on the subscription
solution: Register the Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration resource provider; check AKS cluster health; examine Azure Monitor activity log

symptom: Kubernetes marketplace offer fails: payment instrument not supported; offer cannot be purchased in region; or legal terms not accepted
rootCause: Subscription billing constraints, regional availability restrictions, or legal terms not accepted
solution: Verify subscription billing configuration; check offer regional availability; accept legal terms via Azure portal, CLI, or PowerShell

symptom: Kubernetes marketplace offer: Extension failed to deploy with Internal server error; or Failed to install chart from path for release
rootCause: Extension resource installation failure or incorrect Helm chart values in ARM template
solution: For internal server error: delete extension resource and reinstall. For Helm chart failure: verify values in Azure portal ARM template deployment

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: FailedMount - failed to get keyvault client: nmi response failed with status code 404
rootCause: NMI component in aad-pod-identity returned error for token request; pod identity not properly configured
solution: Check NMI pod logs; refer to Microsoft Entra pod identity troubleshooting guide; verify pod identity binding and AzureIdentity resources

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: keyvault.BaseClient#GetSecret context deadline exceeded
rootCause: Provider pod cannot access Key Vault due to firewall rules, network policies blocking egress, or network jitter on host network
solution: Add provider pods to firewall allowlist; check network policies; verify node connectivity to Entra ID and Key Vault using test pod on host network with curl

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: StatusCode=400, Identity not found when mounting secrets store volume
rootCause: Incorrect userAssignedIdentityID specified in SecretProviderClass custom resource
solution: Get correct identity: az aks show --query addonProfiles.azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider.identity.clientId -o tsv; update SecretProviderClass

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: StatusCode=403 Forbidden - Public network access is disabled and request is not from trusted service nor via approved private link
rootCause: AKS nodes and Key Vault private endpoint are on different virtual networks; no VNet link or peering configured
solution: Create virtual network link for AKS VNet at private DNS zone (privatelink.vaultcore.azure.net); add VNet peering between AKS and Key Vault VNets; verify with nslookup and nc

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: driver name secrets-store.csi.k8s.io not found in list of registered CSI drivers
rootCause: Secrets Store CSI Driver pods not running on the node where the application pod is scheduled
solution: Check CSI Driver pod status: kubectl get pod -l app=secrets-store-csi-driver -n kube-system -o wide; check pod logs if not Running

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: SecretProviderClass not found when mounting volume
rootCause: SecretProviderClass does not exist in same namespace as application pod or wrong name referenced in volume spec
solution: Create SecretProviderClass in same namespace as application pod; verify volumeAttributes.secretProviderClass name matches exactly

symptom: KEDA add-on fails to start: Failed to get API Group-Resources, EOF error connecting to API server; unable to run external metrics adapter
rootCause: Cluster firewall misconfigured, blocking KEDA from reaching Kubernetes API server
solution: Configure firewall to meet Azure Global required network rules for AKS egress traffic; verify KEDA pods running in kube-system

symptom: KEDA operator pods missing workload identity env vars (AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_FEDERATED_TOKEN_FILE); cannot authenticate to Azure scalers
rootCause: KEDA add-on enabled before Workload Identity was configured; env vars not injected into operator pods
solution: Restart KEDA operator: kubectl rollout restart deployment keda-operator -n kube-system; verify env vars via kubectl describe pod

symptom: Managed namespace: FeatureNotFound when registering ManagedNamespacePreview; or BadRequest requiring feature flag
rootCause: ManagedNamespacePreview feature flag not registered or command entered incorrectly
solution: Register: az feature register --namespace Microsoft.containerService -n ManagedNamespacePreview; verify: az feature show

symptom: Managed namespace: cannot use system namespace names; cannot modify via kubectl - resource managed by ARM
rootCause: Reserved system namespace names blocked; managed namespaces controlled by ARM restrict kubectl modifications
solution: Use non-reserved namespace names; manage through ARM API, Azure portal, or az aks namespace CLI commands instead of kubectl

symptom: Network isolated AKS cluster: image pull fails due to network isolation; cannot pull from ACR
rootCause: ACR cache rules or private endpoint not properly configured; for managed ACR, bootstrap resources may be missing
solution: BYO ACR: verify cache rules and private endpoints. Managed ACR: check bootstrap resources, reconcile cluster if missing. Non-MCR images: create extra cache rules in private ACR

symptom: Network isolated cluster: image pull fails after updating cluster to network isolated or updating private ACR resource ID
rootCause: Kubelet configuration in CSE not updated after cluster update
solution: Reimage the node to update kubelet configuration following the Update ACR ID guide

symptom: Network isolated cluster: managed ACR, cache rules, private endpoints, or private DNS zones accidentally deleted
rootCause: Managed ACR infrastructure resources deleted
solution: Cache rule deleted: delete ACR then reconcile cluster. ACR/endpoints/DNS deleted: reconcile cluster

symptom: NAP/Karpenter nodes not removed - underused or empty nodes remain in cluster longer than expected
rootCause: Pods without proper tolerations, DaemonSets preventing drain, PDBs not correctly set, do-not-disrupt annotations, or locks blocking changes
solution: Add proper tolerations to pods, review DaemonSet configs, adjust PDBs to allow disruption, remove do-not-disrupt annotations, review lock configs

symptom: NAP/Karpenter pod connectivity issues - pods cannot communicate with other pods or external services on Karpenter-provisioned nodes
rootCause: NSG rules blocking traffic, incorrect subnet config in AKSNodeClass, CNI plugin issues (wrong conflist, missing IPs in CNS), or DNS resolution problems
solution: Review NSG rules, verify subnet config in AKSNodeClass, restart CNI plugin pods, check CoreDNS config, verify CNI conflist files in /etc/cni/net.d/

symptom: NAP/Karpenter DNS service IP mismatch - pods cannot resolve DNS or kubelet fails to register with API server on Karpenter nodes
rootCause: --dns-service-ip in AKSNodeClass incorrect or not matching kube-dns ClusterIP; DNS IP not in service CIDR; firewall blocking port 53
solution: Verify --dns-service-ip matches kube-dns ClusterIP, ensure DNS IP within service CIDR, check NSG allows port 53 TCP/UDP, restart CoreDNS if errored

symptom: NAP/Karpenter spot VM unexpected node terminations when using spot instances
rootCause: Azure spot VM eviction due to capacity reclaim or price threshold; insufficient instance type diversity
solution: Use diverse instance types in NodePool CRD, implement proper PDBs, consider mixed spot/on-demand strategies, use workloads tolerant of preemption

symptom: NAP/Karpenter VM creation fails with quota exceeded errors
rootCause: Azure subscription quota exceeded; NodePool CRD limited to few VM sizes increasing quota hit probability
solution: Request quota increases via Azure portal; expand NodePool CRDs to include more VM sizes

symptom: AGIC connectivity issues - ingress has no IP address or HTTP timeout accessing services behind Application Gateway Ingress Controller
rootCause: Application Gateway in stopped state, AGIC misconfigured, backend address pools not matching pod IPs, or AGIC pod errors
solution: Verify app via port-forward first; check ingress events; inspect AGIC logs (kubectl logs -n kube-system -l=app=ingress-appgw); verify App Gateway operationalState=Running; compare backend pool IPs with endpoints; start App Gateway if stopped

symptom: Managed NGINX ingress controller returns HTTP 502/504 gateway errors or significant latency under load
rootCause: CPU exhaustion on NGINX ingress pods; HPA reached max replicas or no nodes available; resource limits misconfigured
solution: Monitor HPA (kubectl get hpa -n app-routing-system -w); check pending pods; configure NginxIngressController CRD scaling (maxReplicas, minReplicas, threshold); default 500m CPU request with no limits

symptom: 401 Unauthorized accessing AKS control plane via external identity providers - token audience/issuer mismatch
rootCause: JWT authenticator audience/issuer URL mismatch with token claims or OAuth misconfigured
solution: Verify iss/aud claims match JWT authenticator config; az aks jwtauthenticator update; ensure outbound HTTPS to IdP

symptom: Token validation failures - invalid CEL expressions in JWT authenticator claim mappings
rootCause: CEL expressions have syntax errors or return wrong types
solution: Test CEL expressions; username must return string, groups string array; include aks:jwt: prefix

symptom: AKS workloads exit with permission denied or function not implemented after configuring seccomp profile
rootCause: Seccomp profile blocks required syscalls (clone, io_uring, clock_settime)
solution: Use Inspektor Gadget audit_seccomp to identify blocked syscalls; change defaultAction to SCMP_ACT_LOG for diagnosis

symptom: PostgreSQL on Azure Files: could not change permissions of directory - Operation not permitted
rootCause: Azure Files uses CIFS/SMB - permissions cannot be changed after mount
solution: Use Azure Disk plugin instead; use subPath property

symptom: PVC Pending - Failed to create file share: 403 AuthorizationFailure with dynamic provisioning
rootCause: Storage account firewall blocks persistentvolume-controller; useDataPlaneAPI blocked by firewall
solution: Use static provisioning; or allow AKS VNET in storage account networking

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - Disk cannot be attached because not in same zone as VM
rootCause: Azure Disk LRS default; disk and node in different availability zones
solution: Use node affinity with topology.disk.csi.azure.com/zone; or ZRS disks; or Azure Files

symptom: Azure Disk mount AuthorizationFailed - no authorization for Microsoft.Compute/disks/read
rootCause: AKS identity lacks permissions on disk in different resource group
solution: Assign Contributor role to AKS identity on disk scope

symptom: Multi-Attach error - Volume already used by pod on different node
rootCause: Azure Disk only supports ReadWriteOnce; can attach to one node only
solution: Ensure disk not mounted by multiple pods on different nodes; use Azure Files for ReadWriteMany

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - UltraSSD_LRS requires additionalCapabilities.ultraSSDEnabled
rootCause: Ultra disk attached to node pool without ultra disk support
solution: Create node pool with --enable-ultra-ssd flag

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - applyFSGroup failed input/output error with many files
rootCause: Kubernetes recursively changes ownership for all files when fsGroup set; slow for large volumes
solution: Set fsGroupChangePolicy: OnRootMismatch in securityContext

symptom: FailedScheduling - node(s) exceed max volume count
rootCause: Node VM size reached maximum disk capacity limit
solution: Add node pool with larger VM size; scale pool; delete unused disks

symptom: Azure File share mount error(2) No such file or directory - pod ContainerCreating
rootCause: No connectivity on port 445: file share missing, NSG blocks, firewall blocks, or FIPS node pool
solution: Verify file share exists; check port 445; check NSG; for FIPS use NFS StorageClass

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) Permission denied - storage account key mismatch after rotation
rootCause: Key rotated but Kubernetes secret still has old key
solution: kubectl edit secret to update base64-encoded key; recreate pods

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) - storage account Selected Networks missing AKS VNET
rootCause: Storage account restricted to selected networks but AKS VNET not whitelisted
solution: Add AKS VNET and subnet to storage account networking

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) via private link - FQDN resolves to public IP instead of private
rootCause: Private DNS zone missing virtual network link for AKS VNET
solution: Create virtual network link for AKS VNET in privatelink.file.core.windows.net DNS zone

symptom: Azure File share mount fails - encryption incompatible with AKS < 1.25 kernel
rootCause: AKS < 1.25 kernel 5.4 only supports AES-128; Maximum security disables AES-128-GCM
solution: Use Maximum compatibility profile or enable AES-128-GCM; AKS >= 1.25 supports AES-256-GCM

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) - security profile without NTLM v2 authentication
rootCause: SMB security profile requires NTLM v2 but disabled
solution: Enable NTLM v2 or use Maximum compatibility profile

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails with wrong fs type when setting uid/gid in mountOptions
rootCause: ext4/xfs do not support uid=x,gid=x mount options
solution: Use securityContext with runAsUser/fsGroup; or init container with chown

symptom: Azure File share mount fails with 'host is down' after storage account key rotation
rootCause: Storage account key changed but Kubernetes secret azurestorageaccountkey still references old key
solution: Update azurestorageaccountkey in Kubernetes secret with base64-encoded new key; redeploy pods

symptom: BlobFuse mount fails errno=404 - Unable to start blobfuse
rootCause: Blob container referenced by PV/PVC does not exist
solution: Verify blob container exists in storage account; ensure PV references correct container name

symptom: BlobFuse mount fails errno=403 - authentication or connectivity issues
rootCause: Kubernetes secret has wrong storage key (key rotation) or AKS VNET not whitelisted on storage account
solution: Verify/update Kubernetes secret key; add AKS VNET to storage account networking

symptom: BlobFuse/NFS 3.0 mount context deadline exceeded or volume ID already exists
rootCause: NSG blocks port 443 (BlobFuse) or ports 111/2049 (NFS); or virtual appliance/firewall blocks traffic
solution: Check NSG allows outbound to storage on required ports; add route bypassing virtual appliance

symptom: NFS 3.0 Blob mount access denied by server
rootCause: AKS VNET/subnet not added to storage account selected networks
solution: Add AKS VNET and subnet to storage account networking firewall rules

symptom: Slow Azure Disk attach/detach operations with in-tree driver kubernetes.io/azure-disk
rootCause: In-tree Azure Disk driver performs attach/detach sequentially; >10 ops on single VM or >3 on VMSS
solution: Migrate to CSI driver (file.csi.azure.com / disk.csi.azure.com)

symptom: BYOK CreateVMSSAgentPoolFailed - KeyVaultNotPurgeProtectionEnabled
rootCause: Key vault created without purge protection enabled
solution: Recreate key vault with --enable-purge-protection true

symptom: BYOK CreateVMSSAgentPoolFailed - KeyVaultAndDiskInDifferentRegions
rootCause: Key vault and disk encryption set are in different Azure regions
solution: Create key vault in same region as disk encryption set

symptom: BYOK disk encryption set permission revoked - must grant permissions to key vault
rootCause: Disk encryption set permission for key vault was deleted
solution: Re-run az keyvault set-policy to grant DiskEncryptionSet access to key vault

symptom: Unable to download helm charts in Azure Arc.
rootCause: The issue is with spec.
solution: Check the YAML file where you have mentioned to download the helm charts from helm repository the spec should be repository in the file.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#150494

symptom: Need to run kubectl commands on windows node in kubernetes cluster.
solution: - Need to get kubectl exe file on the windows machine. - Need to copy the kubernetes config file from master node. - Run the below commands to set the environmental variable for kubectl. Note: Here in C:\kube we have the kubectl.exe and kubernetes config file in windows. $env:Path += ";C:\kube" [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path", $env:Path + ";C:\kube", [EnvironmentVariableTarget]::Machine) $env:KUBECONFIG= "C:\kube\config" [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("KUBECONFIG", "C:\kube\config", [EnvironmentVariableTarget]::User) - After running this commands we can able to use kubectl commands on kubernetes cluster windows node.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#163532

symptom: Fluentd monitoring container keeps getting crashed
rootCause: Fluentd container gets crash with out of memory error because the version 1.2.1 has limit of 100Mi.
solution: The next version 1.3.0 has a fix where the limits of memory increased from 100Mi to 150Mi for the fluentd.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#167634

symptom: Unable to create a PVC on AKS Hybrid. 2 node Azure Stack HCI Cluster AKS Hybrid setup. In failover cluster manager, both Cluster nodes were up and running. MOC agent role in FCM was healthy and running. Customer was unable to create PVC on all workload Clusters in AKS Hybrid. Noticed below errors, Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Warning ProvisioningFailed 9s disk.csi.akshci.com_moc_XXXX_XXXX-XXX_XXXX failed to provision volume with StorageClass "default": rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Location 'MocLocation' doesn't expose any nodes to create VHD 'af10dd84-XXXX_XXX-XXX' on: OutOfCapacity Warning ProvisioningFailed 8s disk.csi.akshci.com_moc_XXXX_XXXX-XXX_XXXX failed to provision volume with StorageClass "default": rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Location 'MocLocation' doesn't expose any nodes to create VHD 'af10dd84-XXXX_XXX-XXX' on: OutOfCapacity Warning ProvisioningFailed 6s disk.csi.akshci.com_moc_XXXX_XXXX-XXX_XXXX failed to provision volume with StorageClass "default": rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Location 'MocLocation' doesn't expose any nodes to create VHD 'af10dd84-XXXX_XXX-XXX' on: OutOfCapacity Normal ExternalProvisioning 5s (x2 over 9s) persistentvolume-controller waiting for a volume to be created, either by external provisioner "disk.csi.akshci.com" or manually created by system administrator Normal Provisioning 2s (x4 over 9s) disk.csi.akshci.com_moc_XXXX_XXXX-XXX_XXXX External provisioner is provisioning volume for claim "prometheus/aks-hci-vhdx" Warning ProvisioningFailed 2s disk.csi.akshci.com_moc_XXXX_XXXX-XXX_XXXX failed to provision volume with StorageClass "default": rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Location 'MocLocation' doesn't expose any nodes to create VHD 'af10dd84-XXXX_XXX-XXX' on: OutOfCapacity
rootCause: The azure stack HCI nodes status and MOC (Microsoft on-premises Cloud) agent all were healthy. CSI pods running in AKS hybrid Workload cluster were healthy. Checked wssdagent service status, that was in stopped state.
solution: Decided to repair MOC agent. In MOC repair we notice that there is a network issue (Then customer confirmed they had few infrastructure level changes wrt. Network). MOC repair was successful. wssdagent service started as running. Deploy the PVC again and it was successful.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#180133

symptom: Customer was facing issue while deploying Google Kubernetes Engine Kubernetes cluster on Azure ARC. Customer was using Google Kubernetes Engine auto-pilot cluster and not the Standard Google Kubernetes cluster. Link -- "Compare GKE Autopilot and Standard | Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) | Google Cloud" As the Azure ARC Onboarding was failing, we checked namespaces running on the cluster by below command. PS C:\Users> Kubectl get namespace Checked the Jobs running on cluster and noticed that the JOB was failing. PS C:\Users> Kubectl get job Described the Job PS C:\Users> Kubectl describe Job <Job Name> Name: cluster-diagnostic-checks-job-XXXXX Namespace: azure-arc-release Priority: 0 Service Account: cluster-diagnostic-checkssa Events: Type Reason Age From Message ---- ------ ---- ---- ------- Warning FailedScheduling 21s gke.io/optimize-utilization-scheduler 0/2 nodes are available: 2 Insufficient cpu, 2 Insufficient memory. preemption: 0/2 nodes are available: 2 No preemption victims found for incoming pod. Normal TriggeredScaleUp 13s cluster-autoscaler pod triggered scale-up: [{https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/subscripify/zones/us-central1-b/instanceGroups/gk3-autopilot-cluster-1-pool-1-b0779772-grp 0->1 (max: 1000)}]
rootCause: Google Kubernetes Engine cluster with Auto-Pilot mode is not supported yet. The failure was due to insufficient resources on the Kubernetes cluster.
solution: Issue was fixed after deploying Google Kubernetes Engine k8s cluster in standard mode.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#180136

symptom: While installing the Azure monitoring agent on an Azure ARC enabled Kubernetes cluster, where the worker nodes were running on Oracle Linux 9.2, the customer encountered an error stating that the nat table could not be initialized in iptables. The error message suggested that the table did not exist and asked if insmod was needed. Upon checking the logs of the ama-logs-rs pod in the kube-system namespace, it was found that the addon-token-adapter container was encountering an error while modifying the iptable rules. The error message indicated that there was an issue while adding rules to the custom chain and that the /lib/modules directory could not be found. The error message also suggested that either iptables or the kernel may need to be upgraded. [di****** ~] () $ kubectl -n kube-system logs ama-logs-rs-*****-hcftq -c addon-token-adapterMICROSOFT SOFTWARE LICENSE TERMSMICROSOFT Azure Arc for Kubernetes__________________________________This software is licensed to you as part of your or your company's subscription license for Microsoft Azure Services. You may only use the software with Microsoft Azure Services and subject to the terms and conditions of the agreement under which you obtained Microsoft Azure Services. If you do not have an active subscription license for Microsoft Azure Services, you may not use the software. Microsoft Azure Legal Information: https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/support/legal/F0928 17:22:04.363325 1 main.go:51] error modifying iptable rules: error adding rules to custom chain: running [/sbin/iptables -t nat -N aad-metadata --wait]: exit status 3: modprobe: can't change directory to '/lib/modules': No such file or directoryiptables v1.8.9 (legacy): can't initialize iptables table `nat': Table does not exist (do you need to insmod?)Perhaps iptables or your kernel needs to be upgraded.
rootCause: During a troubleshooting call, it was discovered that the infrastructure, which was running on Oracle Linux 9.2, did not auto-load the module required for the MSI adapter running on the pod to alter the nat tables on the node.
solution: To resolve the issue, the iptable_nat module had to be manually loaded on every node and the node had to be restarted. The command used to load the module is --> sudo modprobe iptable_nat Check if the module is loaded properly --> lsmod | grep iptable_nat

symptom: vCenter vCENTER is showing as Disconnected within Azure connected to Azure Arc Resource Bridge 'ARC-RES-BRIDGE.
rootCause: The logs generated from the below command indicate the following: az arcappliance logs vmware --ip 10.XX.XX.XXX --username USERNAME --password PASS --address XX-VCENTER.microsoft.com --out-dir C:\\Temp\\Folder  2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00 DEBUG Unable to create Kubernetes client from kubeconfig. Error: kubeconfig not specified2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00DEBUGvsphereProvider: Prepping the vsphere provider2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00 DEBUGvsphereClient: GetClient{'vcenterurl': 'XX-VCENTER.microsoft.com'}2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00DEBUGUnable to determine node IP from fabric. Error: error in provider prep: error getting the vSphere SDK client: ServerFaultCode: Cannot complete login due to an incorrect user name or password.2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00DEBUG Kubernetes IPs are2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00 DEBUG Fabric IPs are [] Analysis or Explanation:        2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00 DEBUG Unable to create Kubernetes client from kubeconfig. Error: kubeconfig not specified This log indicates that the system attempted to create a Kubernetes client but failed because the kubeconfig file, which contains the configuration Information for accessing the Kubernetes cluster, was not specified.           2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00 DEBUG vSphereProvider: Prepping the vSphere provider Here, The system is preparing the vSphere provider, which is responsible for managing virtual machines and other resources in a vSphere environment.           2024-09-30T13:00:39+01:00 DEBUG vsphereClient: GetClient {'vcenterurl': 'XX-VCENTER.microsoft.com'} This log shows that the system is attempting to get a client for the vSphere environment using the specified vCenter URL (XX-VCENTER.microsoft.com).           2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00 DEBUG Unable to determine node IP from fabric. Error: error in provider prep: error getting the vSphere SDK client: ServerFaultCode: Cannot complete        login due to an incorrect user name or password. The system failed to determine the node IP from the fabric because there was an error in preparing the provider. Specifically, it encountered a ServerFaultCode indicating that the login could not be completed due to an incorrect username or password.            2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00 DEBUG Kubernetes IPs are This log indicates that the system was unable to retrieve any Kubernetes IPs, resulting in an empty list.             2024-09-30T13:00:42+01:00 DEBUG Fabric IPs are [] Similarly, the system was unable to retrieve any fabric IPs, resulting in an empty list.     In summary, the logs indicate that the system encountered issues while trying to create a Kubernetes client and connect to the vSphere environment due to missing kubeconfig and incorrect login credentials. As a result, it was unable to determine the node IPs from the fabric and retrieve any Kubernetes or fabric IPs.
solution:
Outdated VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge. Update VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge.

Update VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge

 Azure CLI
 Open Cloud Shell
 az arcappliance update-infracredentials vmware --kubeconfig [REQUIRED] --skipWait
 
 Update VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge with non-interactive mode using VMware credentials as parameters
 
 Azure CLI
 Open Cloud Shell az arcappliance update-infracredentials vmware --kubeconfig [REQUIRED] --address [REQUIRED] --username [REQUIRED] --password [REQUIRED] --skipWait

symptom:
The customer encountered connectivity and permission issues with an Azure ARC-enabled Kubernetes cluster, particularly while attempting to access the Workload tab within the Azure portal. Although the Kubernetes cluster was successfully onboarded to Azure ARC, with all pods in the `azure-arc` namespace appearing healthy and a stable connection achieved using the `az connectedk8s proxy` command, issues arose when interacting with specific portal features.  When the customer attempted to access the Workload tab and entered the service account bearer token, the Azure portal threw an error. Investigation revealed that, instead of utilizing the provided token, the portal attempted to access resources with the customerÆs user ID, which lacks the necessary permissions on the Kubernetes cluster. This caused consistent access errors, affecting the customerÆs ability to view workloads as intended. Workload Tab Access Denied: Unable to open the Workload tab on the Azure portal for the ARC-enabled Kubernetes cluster after entering the service account bearer token. Error Message Displayed: After adding the bearer token, an error appears, indicating insufficient permissions or access issues, as the portal attempts to use the customerÆs user ID instead of the token. 403 Error: Persistent 403 error occurs when trying to access the Workload tab, indicating a permissions issue on the portal side.
'Forbidden (403) deployments.apps is forbidden: User '[****]' cannot list resource 'deployments' in API group 'apps' at the cluster scope.' 
   
 Transient 599 Error: A temporary 599 error appears, often due to proxy settings or network configurations, which may intermittently block the URL.
'Error (599) Unable to reach the API server or API server is too busy to respond. {'message':'Failed to fetch','stack':'TypeError: Failed to fetch\n at https://portal.azure.com/Content/Dynamic/UvHUDxXWE-oN/js:113:24067','isError':true}' 
  
 Similar Issues Across Clusters: The customer and their colleagues experience the same issue with multiple clusters, indicating a widespread problem.
rootCause: Portal Not Using the Right Token: When the customer tries to open the Workload tab, the Azure portal isnÆt using the correct access token. Instead, it uses the customerÆs own account, which doesnÆt have permission to view the information on the cluster. This causes an error.  Bug in Azure Portal: ThereÆs a known issue on AzureÆs end where even a valid access token isnÆt enough to open the Workload tab, and it consistently shows an access denied error.  Proxy Settings: The customerÆs network setup, especially proxy settings, might occasionally block the connection to Azure ARC, leading to the temporary 599 error.  Browser Issues: Sometimes, settings or add-ons in the customerÆs web browser can interfere with connecting to the cluster, which might cause issues accessing certain parts of the portal.
solution:
The 599 error was transient and it is due to customer's proxy settings bocking the url.  Below are some
troubleshooting steps for the 599 error.  Customer's
     firewall/antivirussettings do not impact the endpoints specified in Quickstart:
     Connect an existing Kubernetes cluster to Azure Arc - Azure Arc |
     Microsoft Learn Customer's
     proxy server, if they are behind one, doesn't alter network requests to
     endpoints mentioned above. Try
     accessing the API server URI from a freshly installed browser. This is in
     case the existing customer browser might have extensions installed on it
     that could potentially interfere with any requests.  Troubleshoot
     common Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes issues - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn  The 403 error is a consistent repro, it is a bug on portal
end. Fixed the issue and pushed to a private flight to unblock customer. Home - Microsoft Azure

symptom: OpenSSL component (1.1.1) which part of akv secret provider extension, is no longer being supported and is recognized as a security vulnerability by vulnerability scanner.
rootCause: AKV secret provider extension version 1.5.5 has monitor pod (akvsecretsprovider-arc-monitoring-xxxxxxxxxx-xxxxx) which has Images with Open SSL component.
solution:
Upgrade to next version of 1.5.6 where monitoring pod was removed in the version 1.5.6 az k8s-extension update --cluster-name  --resource-group  --cluster-type connectedClusters --name akvsecretsprovider --auto-upgrade false
 Use Azure Key Vault Secrets Provider extension to fetch secrets into Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn

symptom:
Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters should
have the Azure Policy extension installed
solution:
The reported error indicates that the Azure
Policy extension could not be installed on the Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes
cluster due to a communication issue between the extension agent on the cluster
and Azure services. You need to ensure that the Kubernetes cluster can reach
Azure services. Specifically, verify that the cluster can access the following
endpoints: 
 
  https://management.azure.com  
  https://agent.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com
       
  https://policy.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com 
  also ensure no firewalls or network security
groups are blocking outbound traffic on required ports (typically 443 for
HTTPS).

rootCause:

missing SCC permissions
solution:
To resolve this issue,
grant the necessary permissions and restart the kube-aad proxy pod by following
these steps: 
 Switch to the azure-arc project

 Add the privileged SCC

system:serviceaccount:azure-arc:azure-arc-kube-aad-proxy-saAfter executing these
steps, restart the kube-aad proxy pod to apply the changes.

symptom:
Onboarding
of Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster to Azure Arc is failing with below error.  WARNING:
Downloading helm client for first time. This can take few minutes...

ERROR: The command failed with an unexpected error. Here is the traceback:

ERROR: [Errno 2] No such file or directory:
'/root/.azure/kubectl-client/kubectl'
rootCause:
storage.googleapis.com
is used to download kubectl release in case the kubectl is not present in the
customer environment. It
is observed that the endpoint storage.googleapis.com is being blocked
resulting the onboarding operation is getting failed.  It
is needed for a successful onboarding of Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster
to Azure Arc but the endpoint didn't listed in below article. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-arc/kubernetes/network-requirements?tabs=azure-cloud#details
solution:
From
Azure CLI version 2.68.0 onwards, CLI is downloading the binary
from dl.k8s.io/release for
all cloud envs except mooncake, where the binary is downloaded from mirror.azure.cn. Hence,
we no need to allow endpoint storage.googleapis.com for
onboarding Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster to Azure Arc.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201260

symptom: Windows Server 2022 Images previous to the 2025 11B updates may crash with Bugcheck 11B DRIVER_RETURNED_HOLDING_CANCEL_LOCK
rootCause:
This is a code defect / timing which causes the msquic.sys driver to register an etw session on its driver entry routine. If the module had not been previously loaded / unloaded, when nodes are teardown the driver entry routine will be called causing a registration to occur. This occurs in the context of windows performing etw cleanup code, which finds an etw registration on a list that should be otherwise empty, triggering the Bugcheck.

 The code defect has been resolved in FE and GE. See bugs below.
solution: FE bug: Bug 58533461 [Fe] Ensure that DriverEntry is never called while a thread is attached to a server silo GE Bug: Bug 57359043 Ensure that DriverEntry is never called while a thread is attached to a server silo
sourceRef: ContentIdea#208599
